items.title: CFP: 3rd International Interdisciplinary Conference of the Department of English, University of Zululand 28-30 September 2026 Re-imagining Language, W
items.location: University of Zululand, Ongoye, KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa · University of Zululand
items.excerpt: CFP: 3rd International Interdisciplinary Conference of the Department of English, University of Zululand 28-30 September 2026 Re-imagining Language, Writing, and Literature in English in a Fast-Changing World In an era that has been labelled by scholars as the age of technocracy, AI and post-truth, the spheres of literature and the channels of creative an...
items.fullDescription: CFP: 3rd International Interdisciplinary Conference of the Department of English, University of Zululand 28-30 September 2026 Re-imagining Language, Writing, and Literature in English in a Fast-Changing World In an era that has been labelled by scholars as the age of technocracy, AI and post-truth, the spheres of literature and the channels of creative and critical discourses seem to face a dire future. Society globally is becoming entrapped within an accelerated spiral comprising lowest common denominator discourse, the loss of autonomy, the dissipation of embodied social networks and the forceful commodification of imagination and creativity. Considering these threats, it is vital to create radical acts of imagining and reimagining. These should be rooted not only in the hegemonic technocratic traditions and orthodoxies imposed from the Global North, but also, and more urgently, in the lived textures of local language, memory, and survival. The need to re-imagine language, writing and literature is not only an aesthetic necessity, but a desperate need for the future of meaning and society. In the context of AI for example, there are thriving, often cacophonous discourses debating the extent to which the technological deployment of large language models (LLMs) either enhance or curtail individual expression whether in private contexts of self-articulation or in the more public spheres of formal education. In the sphere of formal education, the issue of ethics has assumed a significant role as reliance on, or use of largely predictive AI-enabled or AI-generated content challenges previously stable notions of academic integrity and authentic authorship. While there seems to be consensus on the need for the 'ethical' use of AI in these contexts, the question that continues to divide commentators is: What exactly constitutes 'ethical' use of content or material that is generated or curated in full or part by another party? What exactly is ethical these days? Other vital questions relate to the dominance of certain languages (European) and what seems to be the increased sidelining of others (African in particular) as well as the unsubtly hidden market-centric orientation of the current drive towards AI in academic writing. These are only a few of the significant puzzles posed by the new forms of language-use inaugurated, imposed and valorised by new forms of technology. This invites scrutiny of the more traditional roles that language plays in embodying culture, collective identity, knowledge-creation and transmission as well as in the codification of epistemologies. Given the marginalisation of indigenous languages and knowledge systems in contemporary Africa over the centuries of colonial domination, reclaiming language as a medium to address unabating local dilemmas is proving to be more daunting than ever. Yet, the rise of social media as de-facto sites of imagination and new forms of expression represent possibilities for cultural expression and practices of literature and knowledge production. Furthermore, the increasing diversity of social platforms spawned by technology has a variety of positive and negative implications for, among other things, the re-imagination of fiction, redefining realism, generating alternate currents of speculation, enforcing modes of stereotyping, unforeseen ontological hallucinations, and even contributing towards the current vivification of propaganda. All in all, there is no simple reductionist approach to interpret the current moment. Considering the urgency of the above, the Department of English at the University of Zululand issues the following call for papers: Re-imagining Language, Writing, and Literature in English in a Fast-Changing World Themes and sub-themes which are encouraged (but not limited to) include: The future of language and literature. Imagination and conflicts/interfaces with reality. Academic ethics, authorship and linguistic agency in the age of AI. Ghost Writing and Co-creation Home, homelessness, journeys and writing. Dreaming and language. Multilingualism and Integration Imaginations in literature as forms of resistance and rebellion. Postcolonial literatures, planetarity and the global technocratic order. Imagination and the narrating of already lived (possibly forgotten) histories. Fading/submerged names of nations, peoples, and things, and the (non)realms of departed/buried ontologies. Cracks in reality and meaning. Re-telling and translations Lost childhoods and lost generational mandates/missions. Non-existence as an outcome of personal and social life. Survival and language. Ecological imaginations and environmental futures. The death of nature/ecological unravellings in literary texts Extinctions and disappearances of forms of literature and narratives. Otherness, adaptation and change Possible subversive/creative uses of AI and technology as imagination tools. Slavery, Exploitation, and AI. Slow writing and slow reading as forms of accelerationist resistance and critique. Furthermore, we open the call to other interpretations of the theme which weave academic insights with creative interpretations, including: Short presentations of lived practice of language imaginings, writers' reflections upon their own creative practice(s) which may blur lines between creative and academic spaces. Approaches to creative writing which may enhance imaginative possibilities. Presentations of short pieces of creative writing which directly respond to the theme/sub-themes. Performances of language reimagining or embodied writing. Provocations/perturbations of the possibilities/impossibilities of language and meaning when situated within structured genres/spaces/histories. Literary manifestoes of a "decline to participate" (in the mode of Lesego Rampolokeng, in 2014) in the dominant futurity narrative (challenging the hegemonic rhetoric, adapt to 4IR or be irrelevant) with an insistence on alternative unlived narratives and subversive explorations of change. We invite all interested parties to submit abstracts of approximately 250 words, with five keywords, by 31 July 2026, to the following three email addresses: allank@unizulu.ac.za / phakathib@unizulu.ac.za / ndlovua@unizulu.ac.za If accepted, a member of the organising team will contact you prior to 31 July 2026. The Organising Team, 3rd International Interdisciplinary Conference of the Department of English, University of Zululand.

items.excerpt: Acta Ludologica (ISSN 2585-8599, e-ISSN 2585-9218) is a double-blind peer-reviewed scientific journal published twice a year in both online and print versions. It focuses on the comprehensive discourse of games and digital games, including theoretical and empirical studies, research results, and their implementation into practice, as well as professional...
items.fullDescription: Acta Ludologica (ISSN 2585-8599, e-ISSN 2585-9218) is a double-blind peer-reviewed scientific journal published twice a year in both online and print versions. It focuses on the comprehensive discourse of games and digital games, including theoretical and empirical studies, research results, and their implementation into practice, as well as professional publication reviews and scientific reviews of digital games. Acta Ludologica is inviting manuscripts for Vol. 9, No. 2 , scheduled to be published in December 2026. The deadline for submissions has been extended to July 31, 2026 . Journal issues include scientific reflections on digital and non-digital games, and related media and digital competencies, their creation, design, development, research, history, sociology, psychology, marketing, and the future associated with phenomena of the digital-gaming sector. The journal' regular issues are not thematically oriented, so this CFP is general for manuscripts on any topics related to games and digital games. Acta Ludologica's sections: game studies, reviews, add-ons More information about the individual journal sections, instructions for authors, as well as templates are available in the journal's Guidelines. Submitted manuscripts should strictly follow the outlined format as well as the journal's Citation Rules. Submissions are accepted only in English, with no publication fees, article-processing charges (APCs), or article submission charges. Contact and manuscript submissions: actaludologica@fmk.sk Journal's web and open access archive: www.actaludologica.com Acta Ludologica is indexed in ERIH Plus, ICI Journals Master List, Ulrich's Periodical Directory, CEEOL, and CEJSH. In addition, the journal is under the indexing process for the Emerging Sources Citation Index (ESCI) - Web of Science Core Collection.

items.title: Ecocriticism and Representations of the Sea in the Arts in Portugal
items.location: Lisbon, Portugal · Alyse Kaweng Fan, Thales Reis Alecrim
items.excerpt: The sea and marine life are often considered as pillars of cultural heritage, identity, and collective memories in Portugal (Lourenço 2001; Lourenço 2014). From the historical milestones of the sixteenth century transoceanic navigation (Cortesão 1978) to literary production (Avelar 2024), the ocean has shaped the national imagination of Portugal (T...
items.fullDescription: The sea and marine life are often considered as pillars of cultural heritage, identity, and collective memories in Portugal (Lourenço 2001; Lourenço 2014). From the historical milestones of the sixteenth century transoceanic navigation (Cortesão 1978) to literary production (Avelar 2024), the ocean has shaped the national imagination of Portugal (Tomás 2013). As Antonio Barreto writes: "Heritage is a human creation and the results from history and culture. But there are natural realities that are transformed, through history, culture, and technology into heritage. Such is the case of the sea for the Portuguese" (2019: 15). As a form of heritage, this connection extends to gastronomy and, in a particularly profound way, to the musical sphere (Simões 2013: 45-62; Sardo 2013: 44-64). From fado, with its narratives of longing and loss, to the songs that gave rhythm to the the fishermen's daily work, the Portuguese musical repertoire has functioned as a living archive of human interaction with the environment. However, there is nothing natural or essentialist about this Portuguese relationship with the sea, for it is an imaginary that has been constructed and continously negotiated across historical periods, which also bears the imprint of colonialism (João 2015: 119-147; El-Shawan Castelo-Branco et al 2003: 73-142). More dramatically, the transformations driven by climate change and unsustainable industrial fishing in the twenty-first century threaten not only biodiversity (Ramos-Pereira 2004: 26-43), but also the cultural practices linked to it - a topic that still requires greater attention in schoarly research. In any case, beyond its material and economic dimensions, the sea in Portugal remains a space of imagination and transformation. As evoked in the songs of José Afonso, the sea is not just a site of identity and homecoming, but also a place of alterity and utopia. By singing of the sea, we evoke the possibility of a new era, in which culture and nature meet once again in a shared project of humanity - a vision that also resonates with the notion of the Blue Humanities (Mentz 2023; Oppermann 2019: 443-461). This call seeks to explore the intersection between what we have inherited and what we must protect. Therefore, we invite researchers, artists, and cultural practitioners to reflect on how these environmental changes reshape the meaning of the arts and their role as vehicles of identity and resistance. Papers are welcome on the following topics, amongst others: The sea as a cultural construct in Portuguese music and the arts; Music, critique, and resistance to climate crises in Portugal; Intersections between literature, music, and environmental heritage; Representations of the sea and music in literature and written culture; Music, sound, identity, and the politics of ocean memory; Collective memory, local identities, and soundscapes; The sea in film, the media, and popular culture; Folklore, folk groups, and the preservation of intangible cultural heritage; Coloniality and decoloniality of contemporary maritime identity; Transcultural and postcolonial perspectives on the maritime imaginary; Blue Humanities and music as an environmental archive; Resistance, utopia, and sound activism in the face of climate change; Tourism and heritage preservation of coastal musical cultures; Technology, marine sciences, and music; Ocean ethics, music, and maritime imaginaries; Phenomenology, aesthetics, listening, and maritime experience. Submission Guidelines Proposals should be sent to the email: cecc.fch@ucp.pt, and include paper title, A 300-word abstract in Portuguese or in English should be submitted for a 20-minute presentation, including a provisional title, a brief biographical note (max. 150 words) and contact details. Contributions may be in Portuguese or English. Abstract submission deadline: July 31, 2026 Notification of acceptance: September 15, 2026 After acceptance of abstracts, participants must register for the conference. Registration Fees and Deadlines The registration fee includes coffee breaks and conference materials. Early bird registration (September 16-30): €75 (student) | €100 (non-student) Regular registration (October 1-20): €100 (student) | €150 (non-student) Keynote Speakers Diana Gonçalves, Catholic University of Portugal Susana Sardo, University of Aveiro Organizers Alyse Kaweng Fan e Thales Reis Alecrim Scientific Committee Ana Matoso, CECC Ana Margarida Abrantes, CECC Carla Ganito, CECC Joana Moura, CECC Mário Avelar, CECC Nuno Amado, CECC Patrícia Anzini, CECC Pedro A. Mendes, INET-md Rita Bueno Maia, CECC Sofia Vieira Lopes, INET-md This activity is part of the 4ELEMENTS project, representing the element Water, funded by the Transform4Europe (T4EU) alliance, in a strategic collaboration between the Catholic University of Portugal, the University of Sofia (Bulgaria) and the University of Silesia (Poland).

items.fullDescription: Queer Theory in the Age of Global Asias: An International Conference December 11-12, 2026 University of Hong Kong In the last two decades, queer theory has shifted from theoretical focuses on shame, performativity, and identification to questions of transnationalism, diaspora, queer liberalism, homonormativity, and homonationalism. Simultaneously, when US-based queer theorists (Eng, Ferguson, Gopinath, Munoz, etc.) devised new models such as queer of color critique and queer diaspora to name the alternative subjectivities and telos of modernity beyond queer liberalism, the AsiaPacifiQueer (APQ) network based in Australia collaborated with queer scholars in Asia to organize the First International Conference of Asian Queer Studies in Bangkok in July 2005.

items.fullDescription: The Northeast Popular Culture Association (NEPCA) Politics, Civic Life, and Pop Culture Area invites submissions for NEPCA's annual conference to be held online from Thursday, October 15th, to Saturday, October 17th, 2026. We encourage panel proposals as well as individual submissions. Papers are generally 15-20 minutes in length. We also encourage works in progress, and informal presentations. This area considers the intersection of politics, civic life, and popular culture. Suggested topics include, but are not limited to: Political actions that involve pop culture, including banning or attacking elements of pop culture Depictions of political people, elected officials, voting, political actions and protests, in pop culture Documentary films, television series, or podcasts that focus on politics and civic actions Music and performance that are political in nature The art of political action and protest Depictions of being a "good" citizen versus a "bad" citizen in pop culture Politics, civic life, and pop culture as they intersect with pedagogy and research The call will be open until July 31, 2026. You can submit your proposal at this link. If you have questions about this area, please reach out to the Politics, Civic Life, and Pop Culture Chair: Dana Gavin, Dutchess Community College, dana.gavin@sunydutchess.edu.

items.title: Minor Forms in African Culture
items.sourceName: UPenn CFP: Humanities Computing and the Internet
items.excerpt: In Africa and the African diaspora, knowledge is often produced, circulated, and critiqued in “minor forms”—small-scale oral, visual, material, and digital ephemera such as proverbs, folktales, rites, images, social media, and other non-canonical modes of expression that structure everyday cultural life but are frequently marginalized within dominant acad...
items.fullDescription: In Africa and the African diaspora, knowledge is often produced, circulated, and critiqued in “minor forms”—small-scale oral, visual, material, and digital ephemera such as proverbs, folktales, rites, images, social media, and other non-canonical modes of expression that structure everyday cultural life but are frequently marginalized within dominant academic and cultural hierarchies. While “major forms” such as the literary canon, the national archive, and peer-reviewed scholarship obscure the complexity and vitality of African cultural production, minor forms interact with, resist, coexist alongside, and reshape those dominant modes.

items.title: CFP: From Blueprints to Praxis: Practical Disruption in Academia and Beyond
items.excerpt: Often, when thinking of academia, the ideas of books, writing, and theory are what make up the idea of the scholar. Many scholars who speak about activism, liberation, mutual aid, living in a collective community, and many other topics of concern for the JOAA team, do not always live the practice they write. This year, we are hoping to disrupt this focus...
items.fullDescription: Often, when thinking of academia, the ideas of books, writing, and theory are what make up the idea of the scholar. Many scholars who speak about activism, liberation, mutual aid, living in a collective community, and many other topics of concern for the JOAA team, do not always live the practice they write. This year, we are hoping to disrupt this focus on the blueprints of freedom and look into the way folks are working in the world to build, support, create, and live praxis in their lives.

items.title: Call for Papers: The Erotic Today
items.excerpt: The erotic is a point of infinite signification, a navel in humanity’s symbolic circuit. However, despite its resistance to formalization, it is always in the process of not being written. As Octavio Paz insists, the erotic is a metaphor indelible to the human. As such, it is unsurprising that the manifestations of the erotic in subjective embodied experi...
items.fullDescription: The erotic is a point of infinite signification, a navel in humanity’s symbolic circuit. However, despite its resistance to formalization, it is always in the process of not being written. As Octavio Paz insists, the erotic is a metaphor indelible to the human. As such, it is unsurprising that the manifestations of the erotic in subjective embodied experience are variable and correspond to equally plural treatments of it across the academic panorama. This diverse archive is bound by certain distinguishable threads, in terms of the potentiality of the erotic, its singular relation to language, and to the sphere of sexuality.

items.title: Smell Studies Journal Call for Papers
items.excerpt: CFP: Smell Studies Journal General Editors: Will Tullett, University of York and Manon Raffard, University of Manchester. Editorial board: Andrew Kettler, Chanelle Dupuis, Jayanthan Sriram, Ally Louks, Hsuan Hsu, Sandra Barré. Despite a proliferation of interdisciplinary grant-funded projects on smell, several international conferences dedicated to olfact...
items.fullDescription: CFP: Smell Studies Journal General Editors: Will Tullett, University of York and Manon Raffard, University of Manchester. Editorial board: Andrew Kettler, Chanelle Dupuis, Jayanthan Sriram, Ally Louks, Hsuan Hsu, Sandra Barré. Despite a proliferation of interdisciplinary grant-funded projects on smell, several international conferences dedicated to olfaction, extensive interest from within and outside academia, and a growing literature - the field of Smell Studies is not currently served by its own English-language journal. Smell Studies Journal will be the first international peer-reviewed journal devoted to the field of interdisciplinary Smell Studies. The journal's goal is to promote new research and foster intellectual exchange in the growing field of Smell Studies, which encompasses work across disciplines such as history, geography, anthropology, art history, literature, architecture, sociology, philosophy, environmental humanities, and practice-based arts and performance research. The journal, which will initially have one issue per year, will publish research articles, book reviews, as well as shorter opinion pieces and creative work. Research articles will be evaluated through an open peer review process by members of scientific boards and the editorial board. We would also seek to welcome special issues should such proposals be forthcoming. Pairing academic analysis and theoretical reading with the increased interest in smell and scent in public, the Smell Studies Journal aims to sustain the evolving community of scholars working in the field. Smell Studies Journal will focus specifically on olfaction as a critical object of inquiry, welcoming all approaches from across the humanities and humanistic social sciences. Unlike vision or sound, smell resists representation and archiving, raising methodological challenges that demand a dedicated space for scholarly debate. Olfaction is also irreducibly political: scholarship on smell has illuminated inequalities and injustice in ways that other sensory approaches have not. The journal welcomes contributions from across the humanities and humanistic social sciences, including but not limited to history, cultural history, literary studies, art history, aesthetics, cultural studies, environmental humanities, heritage studies, and curatorial and museum studies. While the journal does not exclude work from the natural or cognitive sciences, submissions from those fields are expected to engage substantively with humanistic frameworks and critical theory. The journal publishes peer-reviewed research articles, opinion essays and interviews, creative works, and reviews of books and exhibitions. We are currently planning the first few issues of the journal. These will appear in a prospectus to a leading academic press who have solicited a proposal for consideration. If we are successful in getting the journal a home then it is likely the first issue will appear in early 2028. As such, this is an initial call for articles, opinion essays, creative work, and reviews of books and exhibitions. We are accepting abstracts for research articles, and ideas for reviews, opinion pieces, creative work, and interviews. Specifically, for research articles please submit a title, 250-word abstract, and either a short biography or a link to where such a biography can be found online. For all other work, or to gauge interest in a research article prior to sending an abstract please e-mail smellstudiesjournal@gmail.com. Initial submissions of abstracts and initial inquiries for the first few issues are requested by the deadline of 1st of August 2026. Continued submissions beyond that date are also open for future issues and for general interest in Smell Studies Journal. Completed submissions for the first issue would be requested for January 1 of 2027, with reviews and editing in Spring and Summer of 2027.

items.title: [The Association for Asian Studies] Racialization, ethnicization, care, and entangled colonial and imperial systems and epistemologies in Asia and the
items.excerpt: This is a CFP seeking panelists for the 2027 Annual Conference of the Association for Asian Studies. We invite papers that examine racialization and ethnicization across Sinophone and Asian contexts in which local projects of improvement and rule are entangled with colonial and imperial systems and epistemologies, both Western and non-Western, external an...
items.fullDescription: This is a CFP seeking panelists for the 2027 Annual Conference of the Association for Asian Studies. We invite papers that examine racialization and ethnicization across Sinophone and Asian contexts in which local projects of improvement and rule are entangled with colonial and imperial systems and epistemologies, both Western and non-Western, external and internal, often sustained by local elites and intermediaries. We understand these contexts in broad terms, including transnational, diasporic, and colonial aspects. Processes of racialization and ethnicization, as well as resistance to and negotiation of such processes, could involve, but are not limited to, discussions of care, gender, and sexuality.

items.title: RSA 2027: Terror and Transcendence: Hybridity in the Renaissance
items.excerpt: Appropriately for a word broadly defined as "having a mixed character," the idea of "hybridity" has elicited contradictory interpretation since its inception in the Greek word hybris. While the word could mean miscegenation, shame, wantonness, outrage, and even violence, the term carried the alternate but equally valid connotation of thinking and moving b...
items.fullDescription: Appropriately for a word broadly defined as "having a mixed character," the idea of "hybridity" has elicited contradictory interpretation since its inception in the Greek word hybris. While the word could mean miscegenation, shame, wantonness, outrage, and even violence, the term carried the alternate but equally valid connotation of thinking and moving beyond human limitations. Despite a shift from classical beliefs through Christianity, hybrid figures such as the centaur and satyr continued to proliferate throughout the Renaissance, evoking both wonder and terror. While sometimes considered blasphemous, these classical hybrids were also allegorized to fit into Renaissance ethical and religious frameworks. At the same time, the early modern engagement with hybridity foreshadowed its place in postcolonial thought, where the hybrid negotiates questions of cultural, gender, and racial identity. This interdisciplinary session will consider how the varied deployment of the hybrid throughout the Renaissance both preserved ancient understandings of the concept while also acting as a preamble to our own contemporary usage. Possible approaches include discussions of Renaissance hybridity as it pertains to: literature and literary theory visual arts geography tracts and/or travel narratives religion and/or mythology theater and performance scientific and/or medical texts education news pamphlets and broadsides classical reception contemporary critical theory Please send a 200-word maximum abstract and 2-page CV to Claire Sommers (claire.sommers@wustl.edu) by August 5, 2026.

items.fullDescription: This panel seeks to explore the precariousness of media, from its physical integrity and digital ephemerality to the complications of human-machine interaction and the elided histories of players, users, fans, and tech developers. With the current encroachment of generative AI in the workplace, media production, information circulation, fan reception, real estate, and the environment, it becomes urgent to question how precariousness underlies the development, deployment, and dissipation of past media.

items.fullDescription: International Conference The Artificial Mind: Art and Philosophy after AI 24-25 September 2026 National Gallery of Art 22 Konstitucijos Ave. Vilnius, Lithuania The title “Artificial Mind” references Claude Lévi-Strauss’ The Savage Mind (La Pensée sauvage (1962) , a foundational work in anthropology, in which he argues that “savage” and “civilized” thought share the same epistemological architecture – differing not in structure but in mode of operations. While the “civilized” engineer works with purpose-built concepts and tools toward a specific end, the “savage” bricoleur thinks with whatever is at hand, constructing new meanings from fragments.

items.title: SCMS-27 Panel: Spatial Historiographies of New York City Cinema after the 1970s
items.excerpt: Conventional cinematic views of New York City are often characterized by aerials, panoramas, and long shots of the archetypal Manhattan skyline, montage of crowded sidewalks, and quick shots of the yellow cabs in congested traffic. Scholars have regularly linked such city views to their historical urban contexts: studies of city symphonies emphasize the c...
items.fullDescription: Conventional cinematic views of New York City are often characterized by aerials, panoramas, and long shots of the archetypal Manhattan skyline, montage of crowded sidewalks, and quick shots of the yellow cabs in congested traffic. Scholars have regularly linked such city views to their historical urban contexts: studies of city symphonies emphasize the concomitance of capitalist modernity, scholars of film noir associate the genre with Cold War anxieties magnified by urban disorientation, while studies of Blaxploitation link the films to the immediate precedents of urban crisis in the 1960s and 1970s.

items.title: Autism at the End of the World: Speculative and Dystopian Takes on Neurodivergence
items.location: Society for Disability Studies 2027 Virtual Conference
items.excerpt: Autism did not exist at the beginning of the world. Who we would now recognize as autistic people existed, yes. But autism itself is undoubtedly new, born in the 20th century clinic and doggedly taken up in popular discourse ever since. Autism did not exist at the beginning of the world, but now, it's hard to imagine a world without it. Despite, or perhap...
items.fullDescription: Autism did not exist at the beginning of the world. Who we would now recognize as autistic people existed, yes. But autism itself is undoubtedly new, born in the 20th century clinic and doggedly taken up in popular discourse ever since. Autism did not exist at the beginning of the world, but now, it's hard to imagine a world without it. Despite, or perhaps because of this, autistic characters frequently feature in speculative and post-apocalyptic media—stories which take place at the end of the world but are often based on or extend from of our own reality.

items.title: Human-Machine interfacing in Western and Global Literature and Media (abstracts and proposals only)
items.excerpt: Human machine interfacing can be as simple as pressing a button or as complex and entangled (also entangling) as inserting a brain chip into the skull. science fiction. Humans with implants, prosthetic and hybrid humans with electronic and mechanical extensions are yet another level are no longer science fiction. Yet, human-machine interfacing has started...
items.fullDescription: Human machine interfacing can be as simple as pressing a button or as complex and entangled (also entangling) as inserting a brain chip into the skull. science fiction. Humans with implants, prosthetic and hybrid humans with electronic and mechanical extensions are yet another level are no longer science fiction. Yet, human-machine interfacing has started long before the invention of these technologies. Machines did communicate with us and tell us about themselves even before chatbots were invented. The clanking of a hammer on the anvil tells us that it is old and dull. The whistle of a microwave tells us that dinner is ready. The process of interfacing is not a one-way process. Through it, humans also communicate with the machine.

In the past three decades, Scream has become a franchise of seven films and a television series (2015-16, 2019). Scream has also expanded beyond the screen, reverberating throughout the popular culture landscape and serving as the foundation for a game, collectible figures, multiple Ghostface mask variations, and an entire collection of merchandise at Spirit Halloween (and elsewhere).

items.fullDescription: Embodied Ecologies and Sacred Landscapes: Interpreting Indian Knowledge Tradition on Island Spaces (ISOIS 2026) December 14-15, 2026 Concept Note: Indian knowledge traditions have long imagined land, water, and life-worlds as sacred, relational, and more-than-human, weaving together rituals, narratives, cosmologies, and practices of care that exceed modern disciplinary boundaries. Island spaces—whether riverine sandbars, coastal archipelagos, sacred groves on hill-islands, or temple-tanks experienced as spiritual islands—offer particularly rich terrains for exploring embodied ecologies, ritualised forms of dwelling, and ecological memory.

items.excerpt: One hundred years: still, the fire burns. It changed everything. Now, we strike the match, one century later. FIRE!!! returns to publish twenty-one new pieces,...
items.fullDescription: One hundred years: still, the fire burns. In 1926, Wallace Thurman, Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, and Aaron Douglas lit a match called FIRE!! , a journal by younger Black artists who refused to wait for permission. It ran one issue. It changed everything. Now, we strike the match, one century later. FIRE!!! returns to publish twenty-one new pieces, paid, in the spirit of the original. There are seats at this table for emerging African American writers, poets, photographers, makers, and artists.

items.fullDescription: Green Day has often been treated as a problem in the “longview” of punk: too popular, too melodic, too commercial, too successful. Green Day matters precisely because the band’s career makes it impossible to separate punk from the mass-cultural conditions it has so often claimed to resist.

items.title: CFP: ANATOMIES OF ANOMALY TRAJECTORIES OF DEFORMITY ACROSS LITERATURE, PERFORMANCE, AND CLINICAL DISCOURSE
items.excerpt: "[...]anomal ne signifie autre chose qu'insolite, inaccoutumé. On doit donc entendre d'une manière générale, par un être anomal, un être qui s'éloigne par son organisation de la grande majorité des êtres auxquels il doit être comparé[...]" (Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, Histoire générale et particulière des anomalie...
items.fullDescription: "[...]anomal ne signifie autre chose qu'insolite, inaccoutumé. On doit donc entendre d'une manière générale, par un être anomal, un être qui s'éloigne par son organisation de la grande majorité des êtres auxquels il doit être comparé[...]" (Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, Histoire générale et particulière des anomalies chez les hommes et les animaux , 1837) Conceived as the concluding conference of the PRIN project Tea-Med - Theatre and Medicine, Anatomies of Anomaly: Trajectories of Deformity across Literature, Performance, and Clinical Discourse aims to explore the category of the anomalous through its historical, cultural, medical, literary, and performative articulations. Drawing on the definition formulated by Isidore Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire - for whom the anomalous coincides with that which appears "unusual" or "uncommon," removed from the majority of bodies and forms deemed regular - the conference aims to expand this category to encompass everything that exceeds normative paradigms, escapes fixed classifications, and resists the systems of control to which it is subjected. In this sense, the anomalous does not merely designate bodily or physiological deformity, but also every form of alterity that destabilises the boundaries of the familiar: identities perceived as eccentric in relation to dominant models; female subjectivities that exceed or subvert historically imposed social and cultural roles; linguistic, narrative, and textual singularities; excluded or marginalised voices; experiences of madness, trauma, and disability; subversive literary and editorial practices; and bodies and minds that challenge the very notion of "normality". As Rosemarie Garland Thomson observes, "The unexpected body fires rich, if anxious, narratives and practices that probe the contours and boundaries of what we take to be human" (Freakery, 1). The conference therefore aims to interrogate those forms of representation, narration, and discourse that have contributed - and continue to contribute - to destabilising and reshaping the category of the human itself; discourses, cultural practices, and modes of knowledge that challenge the boundaries of the representable, the intelligible, and the normative. From this perspective, the conference seeks to foster an interdisciplinary dialogue among scholars working in the history of medicine, literary and linguistic studies, theatre and performance studies, disability studies, gender studies, queer studies, philosophy, psychoanalysis, cultural history, visual arts, and related fields. Particular attention will be devoted to theatre as a privileged site for the exhibition, negotiation, and representation of anomaly: a space in which the deformed, pathological, eccentric, or marginal body is observed, medicalised, spectacularised, or rewritten through new narrative and political possibilities. Possible topics include, but are not limited to: • The monstrous body: teratology, eugenics, and studies of physical alterity • Anomalous minds: madness, trauma, and psycho-physical pathologies • Disability as anomaly: historical, social, and medical representations of disability • Gendered anomaly: queer bodies, performativity, and non-normative identities • Anomaly as social role: disguise, cross-dressing, and the subversion of gender norms • Spectacles of anomaly: freak shows, circuses, and fairs • Anomalous language: linguistic, narrative, dramaturgical, and textual anomalies • The anomalous as archive: case studies, medical documentation, archival gaps, and recording practices • The anomalous as a tool of social control: medicalisation, marginalisation, and the disciplining of bodies • Disturbing anomalies: the grotesque, the pathological, and the uncanny • Anomalous performances: voice, gesture, stage presence, and eccentric corporeality • The anomalous in visual and media cultures: visual arts, cinema, media, and performance • Anomalous spaces: ecologies, environments, and landscapes of deformity • Spiritual anomalies: possession, ecstasy, and altered states • The anomalous beyond the human: posthumanism, alterity, and the redefinition of the norm • Anomalous societies: non-Eurocentric cultural practices and social structures Submission Guidelines The conference welcomes contributions spanning all historical periods and geographical contexts, with a particular emphasis on comparative and interdisciplinary approaches. The call for papers is open to PhD students and early career researchers interested in contributing to the conference discussion. Proposals, in Italian, English, or French, must be submitted by 15 August 2026 to convegnoanomalie.unifi@gmail.com. Submissions should be sent as a .pdf or .docx file named "Surname_AnatomiesConference" and must include the title, an abstract (maximum 300 words), 5 keywords, 5-7 bibliographical references relevant to the proposed topic and a short bio-bibliographical note (maximum 150 words). Authors are also required to specify their institutional affiliation, disciplinary field, and the language of their presentation (Italian, English, or French). All proposals will be evaluated by the Scientific Committee. Notification of acceptance will be communicated by 30 September 2026. Each speaker will have 20 minutes for their presentation. The conference will take place in person on Thursday 26 and Friday 27 November 2026 at the University of Florence. Further details regarding the venue and conference programme will be provided upon confirmation of participation. Participation in the conference is free of charge and accommodation expenses for one night will be reimbursed. Participants are responsible for their own meals and travel costs.

items.title: Dalit Women and the Politics of Labour: Caste, Gender and Invisibility
items.location: Saundarya and Durba, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi
items.excerpt: Labour is deeply intertwined with caste, gender and class in the socioeconomic fabric of India. However, caste and gender are the two factors that remain ignored in this discourse. In the Hindu social order, the caste system assigns vocations through arbitrary hierarchy, producing pernicious structures of marginalisation. Labour performed by Dalits is oft...
items.fullDescription: Labour is deeply intertwined with caste, gender and class in the socioeconomic fabric of India. However, caste and gender are the two factors that remain ignored in this discourse. In the Hindu social order, the caste system assigns vocations through arbitrary hierarchy, producing pernicious structures of marginalisation. Labour performed by Dalits is often stereotyped through casteist assumptions that naturalise certain kinds of work as “belonging” to these peripheral communities. These stereotypes that are perpetuated historically, culturally and socio-politically, are furthered through mechanisms of caste that operate in the material and affective realms.

items.excerpt: November 4, 5, and 6, 2026 - Fluminense Federal University (UFF) Submission deadline: August 16, 2025 Theme: Journalism, democracy, and professional practice: the role of research in contexts of disinformation Registration: https://bit.ly/ 4gCKS9M The Brazilian Association of Journalism Researchers (SBPJor) will organize, between November 4 and 6, the 24t...
items.fullDescription: November 4, 5, and 6, 2026 - Fluminense Federal University (UFF) Submission deadline: August 16, 2025 Theme: Journalism, democracy, and professional practice: the role of research in contexts of disinformation Registration: https://bit.ly/ 4gCKS9M The Brazilian Association of Journalism Researchers (SBPJor) will organize, between November 4 and 6, the 24th National Meeting of Journalism Researchers at the Institute of Arts and Social Communication of the Fluminense Federal University (UFF) in Niterói, Rio de Janeiro. Under the theme "Journalism, Democracy, and Practice," the meeting aims to bring together researchers and students in Communication to debate the impacts of disinformation on journalism research. Papers will be presented in person, and submissions will be accepted solely in Portuguese , following the rules available below: Submission Rules: https://eventos.galoa.com.br/ sbpjor-2026/page/8146-inicio? lang=pt-br We invite researchers, academics, and professionals to submit papers by August 16, via the link: https://proceedings. science/cfp/sbpjor-2026?lang= pt-br The expected date for the announcement of selected papers is September 8, with the registration payment deadline until September 18, 2026. The final program will be announced at October 2 on the event's page (https://eventos.galoa.com.br/ sbpjor-2026/page/8146-inicio? lang=pt-br) and on Instagram (https://www.instagram.com/ sbpjor2026/). JPJor The event will also host the 16th Meeting of Young Journalism Researchers (JPJor) in a hybrid format. Students and graduates in journalism from 2025 and 2026 can submit papers, with up to 5 people in collective authorship and mandatory co-authorship of the advisor. Papers must be written exclusively in Portuguese and contain between 20,000 and 40,000 characters (with spaces), including bibliographic references and footnotes. A title, an abstract of up to 10 lines, and five keywords are mandatory. The text must be written in Times New Roman font, size 12, with 1.5 line spacing. Long direct quotations (more than three lines) must have a 4 cm indent on the left margin, be written in size 10 font, and have single line spacing. For more details, access the link: https://eventos.galoa.com.br/ jpjor-2026/page/8081-inicio? lang=pt-br

items.location: Resurgens Theatre Company / Georgia State University
items.excerpt: Resurgens Theatre Company, along with the Georgia State University Department of English, is pleased to announce our fourth biennial conference on early modern verse drama by Shakespeare and his contemporaries, “Shakespeare’s Apocrypha Now.” We’re calling for papers that examine some aspect of Shakespeare’s non-canonical plays, i.e., those dramatic works...
items.fullDescription: Resurgens Theatre Company, along with the Georgia State University Department of English, is pleased to announce our fourth biennial conference on early modern verse drama by Shakespeare and his contemporaries, “Shakespeare’s Apocrypha Now.” We’re calling for papers that examine some aspect of Shakespeare’s non-canonical plays, i.e., those dramatic works that have been or are currently attributed to Shakespeare (as a whole or in part) that have yet to receive widespread scholarly and/or theatrical recognition. We particularly welcome topics on canonicity, collaboration, and performance-oriented concerns regarding Renaissance drama and/or early modern print culture.

items.fullDescription: We are excited to announce that the Long Middle Ages, a seminar series for postgraduate students and early career researchers working on the Late Antique, Medieval, and Early Modern Periods, will be returning for a second series in Autumn 2026! We welcome submissions of 30 minute papers from postgraduates and early career researchers working in any discipline and on any topic related to the late antique, medieval and early modern periods. Papers will be followed by time for questions and further discussion. Seminars will commence in October 2026 and run on a fortnightly basis until mid-December.

items.fullDescription: Following Yiyun Li's May 2026 Pulitzer Prize win, this session is a critical reception of her memoir as an emancipatory narrative: a descriptive and agential act that represents the unrepresentable experience of maternal grief/trauma. Welcome papers that are interdisciplinary/inter-textual dealing with themes of maternal loss, grief, growth, birth/death, nature/unnatural, death by suicide. Mail 150-200 word abstracts to Dr. Anhiti Patnaik at apatnaik@cascadia.edu Submission Deadline:Monday, August 17, 2026Type:Just-in-Time Session, Modern Language Association Conference 2027Venue:Los Angeles

items.title: CONCEPTUALIZING TRANSNATIONAL FEMINISMS WORLDWIDE
items.location: Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines) Dhanbad, India
items.excerpt: Transnational Feminisms have emerged out of the need for women’s groups to address oppression that crosses national borders and to build solidarities rooted in an exploration of the articulation between the local and the global. These forms of feminisms examine the ways in which women’s groups transcend location beyond national boundaries to address their...
items.fullDescription: Transnational Feminisms have emerged out of the need for women’s groups to address oppression that crosses national borders and to build solidarities rooted in an exploration of the articulation between the local and the global. These forms of feminisms examine the ways in which women’s groups transcend location beyond national boundaries to address their political and ideological struggles, exploring the interrelationships between discrete factors such as caste, class, ethnicity, sexuality, ability, age, and multiple other positionalities and contexts of inequalities as they overlap and are shaped by neoliberal economic and political forces, patriarchies and various other systems of stratification.

items.fullDescription: Embodying the WPA: Advice Narratives for Writing Program Administration Edited by Jackie Hoermann-Elliott, Juliette Holder, Jennifer Judd, & Danielle Littlefield Brady Even among the quietest of us, there are stories to be told. Stories of how we dressed for the campus visit, whether or not we drank wine with the search committee at dinner. Stories from the first year as the WPA, remembering how we physically composed (or contorted) ourselves and our offices for comfort – our own or others’. Stories of how we hugged a bereaved teacher, toasted in celebration, or laughed a little too loud at that one department meeting.

items.title: Engaging Research Across Humanities (ERAH) Graduate Conference
items.excerpt: Date: October 10-11, 2026 Location: SMU Main Campus, Dallas, TX Theme: Movement & Borderlands Abstract Submission Deadline: August 28, 2026 Keynotes: Dr. Tim Bowman & Dr. Elda María Román Graduate students of Southern Methodist University's Departments of English, History, and Anthropology, in collaboration with the Moody School of Advanced...
items.fullDescription: Date: October 10-11, 2026 Location: SMU Main Campus, Dallas, TX Theme: Movement & Borderlands Abstract Submission Deadline: August 28, 2026 Keynotes: Dr. Tim Bowman & Dr. Elda María Román Graduate students of Southern Methodist University's Departments of English, History, and Anthropology, in collaboration with the Moody School of Advanced Studies, Dedman College Interdisciplinary Institute, and the department of English, have collaborated to hold the third annual Engaging Research Across the Humanities (ERAH) Conference at SMU from October 10-October 11, 2026. ERAH 2026 will prioritize the development of interdisciplinary connections and aims to foster a supportive environment for both graduate students and advanced undergraduates engaged in strong research. There will be special emphasis placed on creating opportunities for participants with limited or no prior conference experience. The theme for ERAH 2026 is Movement & Borderlands. If you have any questions, please email arundhatig@smu.edu. Borders simultaneously construct and limit meaning. By demarcating entities, we hold the power to define space and identity. In the same manner, those entities are restricted to oppositional definitions. What does it mean to occupy borderlands, to move across them and through them? How is the deconstruction of borders, or reconceptualization of definitional demarcation, itself an act of meaning-making that exists without opposition? How do borderlands complicate cultural conceptions rooted in duality? How does movement across and through borderlands produce opportunity for new forms of meaning-making not rooted in hard lines and limitations? This year's theme asks us to think critically about the roles of both movement and borderlands in our respective disciplines by considering these questions and others. We invite you to consider the roles of both movement and borderlands within the works you study-be it literary works, historical archives, etc.-as well as how pervasive disciplinary paradigms shape the ways in which you approach your scholarship. Possible topics may include but are not limited to: Migration, mobility, and displacement Citizenship and belonging Racial, ethnic, national, religious identity Borders as constructs Frontiers Artistic and embodied movement Indigenous perspectives on imposed borders Nationalism and borderlands Environmental and ecological borders Linguistic borders Tourism and global circulation We invite abstracts of 200-250 words for both individual presentations and fully-constructed panels (3-4 speakers). Papers in STEM fields that clearly articulate relevance to the theme are welcome. Full-panel proposals will be given priority. Panel proposals must include a 200-250 word proposal for the panel in addition to abstracts for each individual participant. A select number of travel grants will be awarded on a competitive basis for those traveling more than 100 miles. To qualify for a travel grant, you must submit your application by August 14, 2026. Further details will be included in your registration sign-up. Please submit abstracts via the following google form link: https://tinyurl.com/2026ERAH

items.title: The AI Knowledge Society: Mapping Digital Hospitality and Hostility
items.location: Wyndham Atlanta Buckhead Hotel and Conference Center * Atlanta, GA · SAMLA 98: Hospitality
items.excerpt: Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, in contemporary digital society, have drastically evolved from statistical tools to principal instruments of knowledge production, validation and dissemination. Large language models (LLMs), automated search systems, and predictive algorithms increasingly dictate what society knows, how knowledge is validated, and who...
items.fullDescription: Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems, in contemporary digital society, have drastically evolved from statistical tools to principal instruments of knowledge production, validation and dissemination. Large language models (LLMs), automated search systems, and predictive algorithms increasingly dictate what society knows, how knowledge is validated, and who is recognized as an expert/authority. This shift demands a rigorous critique from the perspective of the sociology of knowledge to evaluate how power, inequality, and social structures are embedded within these automated epistemic tools. The panel invites contributions that explore this socio-epistemic duality of AI through conflicting dynamics: Societal Hospitality and Structural Hostility. On the surface, consumer-facing AI presents a welcoming, democratic frontier. It offers immediate information access, bridges linguistic barriers, lowers technical entry requirements, and serves as an affordable intellectual assistant. This interface-level hospitality suggests an era of information democratization, flattening historical institutional gatekeeping. Beneath this user-friendly disguise lies an aggressive political economy of knowledge. This infrastructure relies on the extraction of human cognitive labor, the enclosure of the global intellectual commons, and the automated erasure of marginalized ways of knowing. The back end of AI systems enforces a rigid epistemology that encodes historical social biases, presenting them as neutral, objective, mathematical truths. This panel seeks to bring together scholars, theorists, and empirical researchers to map this friction. The panel aims to explore how AI acts simultaneously as an infrastructure of social integration and a vector of epistemic alienation. The panel invites abstracts from scholars at all career stages, encouraging contributions from digital sociology, science and technology studies (STS), critical race studies, media studies, political economy and other interdisciplinary fields. The panel welcomes empirical, theoretical, and methodological papers that address questions including, but not limited to: The Political Economy of Algorithmic Expertise The Erasure of Tacit and Indigenous Knowledge The Deskilling and Alienation of Cognitive Labor Conversational AI as a Trojan Horse Digital Colonialism and Epistemic Homogenization Algorithmic Stratification and Epistemic Class Divides Epistemic Resistance, Subversion, and Counter-Publics The Techno-Religious Frame: AI as the Modern Oracle Reference: Benjamin, R. (2019). Race After Technology: Abolitionist Tools for the New Jim Code. Polity. Collins, H. (2024). Why Artificial Intelligence Needs Sociology of Knowledge. Cambridge University Press. Crawford, K. (2021). Atlas of AI: Power, Politics, and the Planetary Costs of Artificial Intelligence. Yale University Press. Fricker, M. (2007). Epistemic Injustice: Power and the Ethics of Knowing. Oxford University Press. Liu, C. (2026). The Sociology of AI as an Emerging Field: Mapping Tensions and Boundaries. Sociology Compass. Zuboff, S. (2019). The Age of Surveillance Capitalism. Public Affairs. Submission Requirements: Abstracts: 300-500 words, clearly stating the research problem, theoretical framework, methodology (if empirical), and core argument along with A/V requirements Keywords: 3-5 keywords Biography: A brief biography (100 words) of the author(s)Please send queries, suggestions and scheduling requests to Dr. Amrita Basu Roy Chowdhury (Lady Brabourne College, India) at basuroyamrita1983@gmail.com and/or through the Ballast platform by 31 August 2026. [Please note that all submissions will eventually have to be uploaded through Ballast.]

items.title: Gender in Fantasy
items.location: Dr Kevan Manwaring/The British Fantasy Society
items.excerpt: Fantasy has long explored lifeworlds and paradigms outside of societal norms. Orlando, Virginia Woolf’s gender-fluid protagonist, declares, ‘I'm sick to death of this particular self. I want another.’ World myths, legends, folk tales, and fairy tales are early promoters of gender-fluidity, populated by the likes of Inanna/Ishtar; Hermaphroditus, the offsp...
items.fullDescription: Fantasy has long explored lifeworlds and paradigms outside of societal norms. Orlando, Virginia Woolf’s gender-fluid protagonist, declares, ‘I'm sick to death of this particular self. I want another.’ World myths, legends, folk tales, and fairy tales are early promoters of gender-fluidity, populated by the likes of Inanna/Ishtar; Hermaphroditus, the offspring of Hermes and Aphrodite; androgynous Dionysus; Ardhanarishvara; Ometeotl; Guanyin; cross-dressing thunder and trickster gods; heartsick seafaring maidens disguised as sailors; and the mercurial ontologies of the Fae. In this issue we will explore how gender is portrayed and explored in Fantasy.

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers The Editorial Board of Translation Criticism ( Kritika prekladu ) invites submissions for the 2026 issues of the journal. Extended submission deadline: 31 August 2026 We welcome original, unpublished contributions in the field of Translation Studies and related disciplines. We invite the submission of full-length research papers of up to 36,000 characters (including spaces). The journal accepts submissions in Slovak, Czech, German, Russian, French, Spanish, Italian, Hungarian, and Polish. All submissions undergo a double-blind peer-review process. For further information on the journal's scope, author guidelines, and submission procedures, please visit the journal's website: Translation Criticism - Kritika prekladu https://www.kritikaprekladu.sk/en/translation-criticism/

items.fullDescription: Invitation for Chapter Contributions Book Title: Language, Literature, and Learning: Interdisciplinary Perspectives Edited By Dr. R. Saraswathi Dr. Sumalatha Donipati ISBN: 978-93-92760-14-3 Publisher: KY Publications, India KY Publications cordially invites academicians, researchers, faculty members, research scholars, and postgraduate students to contribute original and unpublished book chapters for the forthcoming edited volume, Language, Literature, and Learning: Interdisciplinary Perspectives. This volume aims to provide an interdisciplinary platform for innovative research exploring the intersections of language, literature, education, linguistics, communication, translation, cultural studies, pedagogy, and contemporary learning practices. High-quality theoretical, empirical, and review-based chapters are welcome. Kindly communicate only through WhatsApp text messages for submission-related queries and updates. items.title: Cultivating Moral Agency: Pedagogies of Museum Ethics
items.location: New York, NY · College Art Association
items.excerpt: Ethical questions have become central to museum work, increasingly shaping how institutions acquire, preserve, interpret, and sometimes deaccession collections. Yet discussions around the pedagogy of museum ethics are underdeveloped. This session will convene scholars and practitioners to collaboratively explore best practices for teaching museum ethics a...
items.fullDescription: Ethical questions have become central to museum work, increasingly shaping how institutions acquire, preserve, interpret, and sometimes deaccession collections. Yet discussions around the pedagogy of museum ethics are underdeveloped. This session will convene scholars and practitioners to collaboratively explore best practices for teaching museum ethics across a range of educational contexts. I invite contributions that consider how educators help students enter ethical conversations and develop their own orientations and convictions. Perspectives may address semester-long courses in museum ethics, focused units embedded within other classes, or museum-based training programs. Relevant topics include repatriation and restitution, provenance research, the care and display of human remains, confronting the legacies of colonization, interpreting sites of tragedy, violence, and/or slavery, or the responsibilities of museums towards descendant and source communities. Contributors are encouraged to share case studies, assignments, classroom strategies, or institutional partnerships and reflective assessments of successes and tensions encountered in teaching ethical issues. This session aims to foster dialogue across disciplinary and institutional boundaries to move toward shared frameworks for teaching museum ethics that support emerging practitioners in imagining and enacting ethical futures.

items.title: NeMLA Panel 2027: Women's Writing and the Question of Canon
items.excerpt: Women's writing emerged as a critical category in response to the historical exclusion of women from literary canons and archives. The recovery of neglected texts, authors, and traditions has transformed literary studies, creating new genealogies of reading and interpretation. Yet the category itself raises important questions. Does the designation "women...
items.fullDescription: Women's writing emerged as a critical category in response to the historical exclusion of women from literary canons and archives. The recovery of neglected texts, authors, and traditions has transformed literary studies, creating new genealogies of reading and interpretation. Yet the category itself raises important questions. Does the designation "women's writing" continue to serve as a productive framework, or does it risk reinforcing the very marginalization it seeks to address by positioning women outside the literary mainstream? As Elaine Showalter argues, feminist criticism should not simply "fit women between the lines of the male tradition" but attend to the cultural and historical conditions that shape women's literary production and reception. Her influential formulation of gynocriticism intends to recover neglected women writers and establish women's writing as a significant field of scholarly inquiry. Subsequent feminist scholarship has complicated these foundations further. Black feminist critics such as Barbara Smith, bell hooks, Hortense Spillers challenge the tendency to treat "woman" as a universal category, while postcolonial theorists including Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak and Chandra Talpade Mohanty question the assumption of a coherent global female experience. Likewise, theorists such as Judith Butler and Jack Halberstam have interrogated the stability of gender itself. Together, these interventions have encouraged scholars to frame women's writing not as a singular tradition but as a diverse and contested field shaped by race, class, caste, sexuality, nationality, language, and other intersecting forms of identity and power. This panel invites papers that reconsider critical strategies for studying women's writing, with particular attention to the reception of women's writing. How do questions of race, class, sexuality, language, nation, and geography influence the ways women writers are read, taught, circulated, translated, archived, and canonized? Topics may include feminist literary criticism, reception studies, archival practices, translation, publishing histories, readerships, pedagogy, and comparative perspectives on women's writing across languages and cultures.

items.fullDescription: La SITA-Società Internazionale Tommaso d'Aquino, in collaborazione con la Pontificia Università della Santa Croce e l'Istituto Tomistico dell'Angelicum, con il patrocinio della CRUIPRO e dell'UCIIM, organizza il convegno di studi Volti di un tomismo vivo: il Novecento Italiano. L'obiettivo è presentare i tomisti italiani del '900 che, con il proprio lavoro, hanno mostrato l'attualità e la multidisciplinarietà del pensiero dell'Aquinate. Il convegno sarà il primo di una serie che presenterà, annualmente, aree geografico-linguistiche differenti. Le proposte di papers devono comprendere: titolo; key words (massimo 5); abstract (massimo 300 parole); bibliografia di riferimento; CV breve. Sono ammessi contributi in lingua italiana e inglese che presentino la figura di un tomista italiano del Novecento. Gli Atti del convegno, in cui confluiranno le relazioni presentate e le altre proposte in linea con il tema del convegno, saranno pubblicati in un numero monografico della rivista Angelicum nell'anno 2027. items.title: Before the Renaissance: Hollywood Art Cinema Under the Studio System
items.excerpt: At its most ambitious, the New Hollywood was a movement intended to cut film free of its evil twin, commerce, enabling it to fly high through the thin air of art. The filmmakers of the '70s hoped to overthrow the studio system, or at least render it irrelevant, by democratizing filmmaking, putting it into the hands of anyone with talent and determination.

items.title: Postcolonial Hospitality
items.excerpt: This panel at the SAMLA 97 conference (Nov. 5-7, 2026) examines hospitality as a critical framework within postcolonial studies, attending to the ways literary and cultural texts negotiate welcome, refusal, obligation, and exposure in the aftermath of empire. Within empire studies, hospitality emerges as a fraught ethical and aesthetic relation shaped by...
items.fullDescription: This panel at the SAMLA 97 conference (Nov. 5-7, 2026) examines hospitality as a critical framework within postcolonial studies, attending to the ways literary and cultural texts negotiate welcome, refusal, obligation, and exposure in the aftermath of empire. Within empire studies, hospitality emerges as a fraught ethical and aesthetic relation shaped by colonial histories, uneven mobility, and asymmetrical power. We consider how such texts register these dynamics across postcolonial contexts. Readings across genres and media—including novels, graphic novels, life writing, film, poetry, and theory—deploy hospitality as a vital lens for understanding postcolonial subjectivity, relationality, and the ongoing legacies of colonial governance.

It also marks the announcement of a film version of one of her most famous and most prophetic works, Parable of the...
items.fullDescription: 2026 marks multiple milestones for the legacy of legendary Afrofuturist author Octavia E. Butler: fifty years since the publication of her first novel, Patternmaster (1976), and the twentieth anniversary of her passing, on February 24, 2006. It also marks the announcement of a film version of one of her most famous and most prophetic works, Parable of the Sower (1993), as well as the surprise republishing of her disavowed novel, Survivor (1978), for the first time since the 1980s. These anniversaries arrive at a moment when Butler’s work continues to gain public attention for its prescience and political relevance, and as she continues to amass scholarly attention across scholarly disciplines.

items.fullDescription: I seek proposals for brief scholarly essays (3500-5000 words) that provide an overview of a chosen aspect of motherhood in the American imagination for a volume under contract with a new Bloomsbury series called Exploring the American Imagination: Ideals, Values, and Myths in Popular Culture. These overview essays should cite a number of popular culture texts to provide an overview of the tensions and contradictions as well as the foundational beliefs inherent in various aspects of American motherhood. Essays should cover multiple genres (film, fiction, television, graphic novels, music) and a broad time period. These are overviews or research starters rather than narrow analyses.

items.excerpt: Virginia Woolf’s Mrs Dalloway (1925) remains a cornerstone of the modernist literary canon. Beneath its seemingly simple plot, the novel exe...
items.fullDescription: Virginia Woolf’s Mrs Dalloway (1925) remains a cornerstone of the modernist literary canon. Often celebrated as Woolf’s greatest novel, the plot is set in post-World War I London and revolves around a single day in the life of its protagonist, Mrs Clarissa Dalloway, as she prepares to host an evening party. Beneath its seemingly simple plot, the novel exemplifies a profound exploration of consciousness, time, and the inner lives of characters through its extensive use of the stream-of-consciousness technique. Although narrated in a third-person omniscient voice, the narrative primarily focuses on the inner consciousness of the characters.

items.title: Déplacements dans les réseaux transcoloniaux : l'Asie, l’Afrique et/ou l’Europe reliés
items.excerpt: Pendant la période de l'Indochine française, les premiers vietnamiens sont acheminés en Nouvelle-Calédonie en tant que travailleurs sous-contrat dans les mines de nickel. Cette période marque en parallèle la rébellion très présente des autochtones de l'île - les Kanak - face à la dépossession territoriale des colons français. Le...
items.fullDescription: Pendant la période de l'Indochine française, les premiers vietnamiens sont acheminés en Nouvelle-Calédonie en tant que travailleurs sous-contrat dans les mines de nickel. Cette période marque en parallèle la rébellion très présente des autochtones de l'île - les Kanak - face à la dépossession territoriale des colons français. Le refus de participer à l'industrie minière d'un groupe favorise l'expatriation de l'autre sur l'archipel en pleine ère de la colonisation française dans plusieurs colonies. Le roi tonkinois Ham Nghi, à l'âge de 18 ans, a été placé en exil en Algérie après des années de mouvements de résistance contre la puissance coloniale française. Son expulsion fait écho à celle de la Reine malgache Ranavalona III, qui a eu lieu après l'annexion de Madagascar par la France en 1896. L'ensemble de l'empire français, étendu de l'Indochine à l'Afrique insulaire, est sujet à la déportation dans la plus grande colonie de peuplement. Ce réseau de déportation des colonies liées met en avant la question des outils de domination employés par le système colonial. Malgré les différentes populations colonisées, la stratégie de domination s'applique d'une certaine façon. **** Dans le cadre académique au sein des études francophones et françaises, nous voyons souvent des pistes de spécialisation distinctes, telles que les études européennes, les études caribéennes, et les études postcoloniales francophones. Les cours d'histoire et de littérature offrent également des réflexions particulières selon une région spécifique ainsi qu'une époque donnée. L'objectif de former les futurs chercheurs dans des domaines précis, faute d'études approfondies sur les caractéristiques transcontinentales, risque de faire oublier qu'au coeur de l'histoire de l'un est tissée celle de l'autre. Cette imbrication transnationale et transcommunautaire, inévitable soit-elle, est due à la fois aux déplacements migratoires, à l'exil d'après-guerre, et à la mobilisation de la force de travail par le système capitaliste et colonial. Lorsque l'on met en évidence ce lien intrinsèque entre des événements, on fait émerger un réseau transcolonial qui lie l'Afrique, l'Asie, et/ou l'Europe. Ce réseau nous oblige à réfléchir à l'interconnexion de l'un à l'autre, d'une communauté spécifique à une autre soi-disant éloignée. Dans le contexte du monde d'aujourd'hui, une telle conscience de l'interconnexion des histoires coloniales peut susciter des réflexions : Peut-on vraiment ignorer le combat de l'autre ? Quels seront les enjeux ? Communications à envoyer soit en anglais, soit en français.

items.title: Migration from Kurdistan and Brain Drain: Factors, Challenges and Prospects
items.location: Univeristy of Kurdistan, KRI, Iraq · Univeristy of Kurdistan
items.excerpt: Call for Papers Migration from Kurdistan and Brain Drain: Factors, Challenges and Prospects (December 1st, 2026) The University of Kurdistan Hewlêr (UKH) invites scholars, researchers, policymakers, practitioners, and members of the Kurdish diaspora to submit abstracts for an international interdisciplinary conference on Migration from Kurdistan and...
items.fullDescription: Call for Papers Migration from Kurdistan and Brain Drain: Factors, Challenges and Prospects (December 1st, 2026) The University of Kurdistan Hewlêr (UKH) invites scholars, researchers, policymakers, practitioners, and members of the Kurdish diaspora to submit abstracts for an international interdisciplinary conference on Migration from Kurdistan and Brain Drain: Factors, Challenges and Prospects. We welcome theoretically informed and empirically grounded papers that examine how brain drain, and brain waste are shaped by conflict and displacement, labor-market pressures, governance and legal frameworks, statelessness, social networks, gendered and generational inequalities, and the health and psychosocial consequences of mobility. Contributions may address nationalism, diaspora identity, intersectionality, return and re-return, remittances and knowledge migration, and the institutional conditions that enable retention, legal protection, and reintegration. The conference aims to advance comparative and policy-relevant debate on migration not only as a question of departure, but also of rights, belonging, development, and future-making in Kurdish society and beyond. This conference aims to bring together scholars, practitioners, and researchers to examine Kurdish migration in its full complexity: its drivers and consequences, its gendered and generational dimensions, and the conditions under which departure can give way to circulation, return, and knowledge transfer. We invite empirically rigorous and theoretically informed contributions from across disciplines, including but not limited to sociology, economics, law, public health, political science, and psychology. Submissions are welcome in English only. We invite potential contributors to submit papers on the following (but not limited to) topics: Intersectionality and migration Skilled Migration: Brain Drain vs. Brain Gain Brain-drain from KRI: factors and incentives Return migration to KRI: challenges and opportunities Legal and political challenges Socio-economic challenges Nationalism, patriotism and the Kurdish diaspora Kurdish diaspora identity and transnational belonging Divisions within Kurdish diaspora: political and cultural disparity Language and cultural loss among second-generation Kurds Psychological context of migration Health and mental health of refugees Higher education Comparative perspectives from other regions and contexts University of Kurdistan Hewlêr welcomes innovative and interdisciplinary approaches to research and encourages submissions that engage with the conference theme from diverse perspectives, including topics not explicitly listed above. Conference Format The conference is planned to take place in Erbil, Kurdistan Region of Iraq, in December 1st, 2026. The deadline for abstract submission is on 01/09/2026 at 12:00 PM (Erbil time, GMT+3) The deadline for full paper submissions is on 01/11/2026 at 12:00 PM (Erbil time, GMT+3) AI Usage Policy To adhere to the best academic practices in research integrity, all submissions must adhere to the following standards concerning AI usage. AI usage, as determined by Turnitin AI detection services, must not exceed 20%, in part or whole of the submitted document. UKH measures large research outputs in either 10- or 20-page increments (splits) when applying this policy. UKH does not recognize any distinction between AI generation and AI editing. Citation and Reference Policy References and citations must be formatted according to the Harvard referencing style. All citations, either direct quotations or paraphrases, must have the exact page number or range of page numbers from which they came, unless stated in an address specifying the scope or coverage of the source's usage. Linked or databased academic articles or online books which have established page numbers and have been published must be cited and referenced independently as a stand-alone document. Submission Guidelines Abstracts should be between 250-350 words and include an abstract title and 3-5 keywords. A brief author biography (200-250 words) must be submitted in a separate file. Please include your full name, affiliation, and contact details (email address) in your biography file. Format Requirements Submit as two separate PDF files: one for the abstract and one for the biography Abstracts must include an abstract title Use Times New Roman, 12pt font, and 1.5-line spacing File names should follow the format: [LastName]_Abstract.pdf and [LastName]_Bio.pdf Abstracts and author biographies should be submitted as separate PDF files via email to migration.conference@ukh.edu.krd For questions, please contact migration.conference@ukh.edu.krd

items.title: Unsettling the Threshold: Asian American Literature, Culture, and Politics of Hospitality
items.excerpt: In her foundational text Immigrant Acts , cultural theorist Lisa Lowe argues that the Asian immigrant exists as a “perpetual foreigner within the body politic,” a figure through whom the nation-state defines its own boundaries and citizenship. For Lowe, Asian American cultural production emerges precisely from this “distance from the national culture,” se...
items.fullDescription: In her foundational text Immigrant Acts , cultural theorist Lisa Lowe argues that the Asian immigrant exists as a “perpetual foreigner within the body politic,” a figure through whom the nation-state defines its own boundaries and citizenship. For Lowe, Asian American cultural production emerges precisely from this “distance from the national culture,” serving as a site that critiques the state’s false promises of universal inclusion.

items.fullDescription: Setting the stage: a theatre and performance dissertation writing group Writing a dissertation can be lonely: we want to change that. Many of us first fell in love with theater because — through exploratory rehearsals, late-night tech runs, and joyful opening nights — we found camaraderie and connection, both of which can feel distant in graduate school. If theater is best practiced with others, how can our research and writing processes be shaped by the same commitment to community-building?
