items.title: Beyond Hegemony: Masculinities in World and Comparative Literature
items.excerpt: This panel examines how literary and cultural texts from diverse global traditions imagine masculinity not merely as an individual identity but as a relational and communal practice. In an era marked by migration, political polarization, social fragmentation, environmental crisis, and renewed struggles for collective empowerment, narratives of masculinity...
items.fullDescription: This panel examines how literary and cultural texts from diverse global traditions imagine masculinity not merely as an individual identity but as a relational and communal practice. In an era marked by migration, political polarization, social fragmentation, environmental crisis, and renewed struggles for collective empowerment, narratives of masculinity have become central sites through which communities negotiate belonging, responsibility, vulnerability, and resistance. While dominant models of masculinity have often been associated with hierarchy, authority, nationalism, and exclusion, contemporary world literatures increasingly depict alternative masculinities grounded in care, ethical responsibility, emotional openness, solidarity, and collective survival. Such representations challenge inherited structures of power while opening new possibilities for community formation and social transformation. This panel invites comparative and transnational approaches that explore how literary texts, films, cultural narratives, and other expressive forms construct, critique, or reimagine masculinities within local and global communities. Particular attention may be given to how gender intersects with race, class, migration, coloniality, religion, ecology, technology, and cultural memory in shaping collective identities and practices of empowerment. Situated within the broader concerns of World Literature and Comparative Literature, this session seeks to foster dialogue across regions, languages, and traditions while investigating how masculinities participate in the creation-or disruption-of empowered communities. We welcome papers addressing, but not limited to: Comparative constructions of masculinity across world literatures Masculinity and migration, diaspora, or displacement Literary representations of fatherhood, mentorship, and communal responsibility Masculinities in Global South literatures Masculinity and environmental or ecological communities Masculinity, memory, and post-conflict reconstruction Queer and non-hegemonic masculinities in world literature Masculinity and cultural resistance under authoritarian regimes Masculinity, religion, and collective belonging Masculinity and affect in contemporary global fiction

items.title: Revisiting the Great War: Women's Voices, Experiences, and Works
items.excerpt: Our session examines the importance of women as fictional characters, authors, and purveyors of legacies associated with the Great War of 1914-1918. It is hoped that we can bring a fresh critical light to this pivotal moment in world history. Too often, scholarly reflection on the Great War tends to focus military activity and various aspects of masculine...
items.fullDescription: Our session examines the importance of women as fictional characters, authors, and purveyors of legacies associated with the Great War of 1914-1918. It is hoped that we can bring a fresh critical light to this pivotal moment in world history. Too often, scholarly reflection on the Great War tends to focus military activity and various aspects of masculine performance. We would like to use a wider lens for our discussion of the Great War. Please note the very wide range of perspectives in this seminar: authors, characters, and context. More precisely, our reconsideration of the works, authors, and legacies of the Great War includes three directions: first, the examination of women appearing as fictional characters in this normally male-oriented thematic area; second, reflection on women writers, artists, and filmmakers engaged in representing the Great War; finally, establishing historical continuities deriving from the relationship between women and the Great War. The flexible methodology of this session includes the examination of novels, poems, paintings, photography, film, documentaries, memoirs, and memorials. A few suggestions for interpretative directions: · Recent critical directions on topics relating to women and the Great War · Non-European, global writing on the Great War · Themes of colonialism, migration, class, public health, punition, sexual violence, and race · The inclusion of documentary, archival, and any relevant internet materials · Presentations on "Women, New England, and the Great War" · The involvement of women in medical and public health issues during the Great War · Women in the Great War as represented in museums, local, national, and global media.

items.title: Subtle Performances of the Body: Female Agency and Autonomy in Literature

Under the antagonistic system where the pursuit of "good life" is no longer plausible, how do women across different cultures, historical periods, and geopolitical contexts respond by utilizing their bodily autonomy an...
items.fullDescription: How do female characters employ their bodies to navigate, process, and challenge the cultural and socioeconomic structures of their times? Under the antagonistic system where the pursuit of "good life" is no longer plausible, how do women across different cultures, historical periods, and geopolitical contexts respond by utilizing their bodily autonomy and agency? This panel invites papers that critically investigate female bodily representation in the context of empowerment and change. In particular, this panel is interested in literary analyses that focus on the subtle yet tangible (dis)orientation of female bodies in and out of public spaces. Gestures, clothing, behavior, decorum, and adherence to social respectability are tactical ways in which female characters maneuver the contours of their precarious belonging to the surrounding environment. We welcome papers that extend the existing scholarship on female agency and bodily autonomy as a crucial means of cultural transformation. This (in-person) panel invites papers that critically investigate female bodily representation in the context of empowerment and change. In particular, this panel is interested in literary analyses that focus on the subtle yet tangible (dis)orientation of female bodies in and out of public spaces. https://cfplist.com/nemla/ Home/S/22387

items.title: "Why Am I Reading This?" Finding Literary Relevance
items.excerpt: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part...
items.fullDescription: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part of our cultural imagination, but it's less easy to coax students into reading Beowulf or Tom Jones -texts that are less familiar in form and content. As we jettison the canon and the timeless truths of literature, what do we expect reading older texts will give our students that they wouldn't otherwise understand? How do we explain our reasoning to students about why they should read texts from earlier literary periods? How do we present these earlier periods (or periodization) and their culture and ideas as relevant to our undergraduate students in our English, writing, or humanities programs? Or is relevance the problem? How might we redefine older literature in our classes, or what we (can) do with these texts? I'm particularly interested in position papers that take into consideration practical pedagogical and theoretical concerns: arguments about curricula, course development or assignments that do different things with this literature. Papers are welcome on any kind of literature/texts or periods prior to 1800.

items.title: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual.
items.location: Salve Regina University, New Port Rhode Island · NeMLA
items.excerpt: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. How...
items.fullDescription: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. However, several personal narratives resist these assumptions. Edited autobiographies, oral histories, collaborative memoirs, and interview-based narratives often construct an autobiographical "I"/protagonist that is multiple rather than singular and that emerges across the voices of individuals whose experiences collectively narrate a shared history. Likewise, some collective life narratives depart from conventional autobiography structures, appearing as fragmented accounts, interwoven testimonies, or dispersed chapters within larger texts. This panel invites papers that examine forms of self-representation that foreground the collective. Particularly of interest are papers that interrogate the presumed unity of author, narrator, and protagonist; reconsider the autobiographical pact in light of collaborative and collective forms of narration; and explore how communities write themselves into history through shared acts of remembrance, testimony, and self-representation. Topics to consider include, but are not limited to: Collaborative and edited autobiographies Oral histories and interview-based life narratives Community and collective testimony Indigenous, Black, and diasporic collective life writing Gender, Sexuality and self testaments Archives, memory, and community self-representation Theoretical reconsiderations of the autobiographical pact and authorial voice Experimental and hybrid forms of autobiography Collective authorship, voice, and narrative authority Life writing, activism, and community formation. By centering collective modes of self-account, this panel seeks to expand the boundaries of autobiography studies and establish interventions that consider how communities, rather than individuals alone, become autobiographical subjects.

items.fullDescription: When Henry James described the Victorian multi-plot novel as “large loose baggy monsters,” he didn’t have twentieth and twenty-first century horror in mind, but the echoing effects of the Gothic on writers like Charles Dickens, Wilkie Collins, Mary Elizabeth Braddon, Charles Reade, and others. These writers drew on the multiplied plots and perspectives of the Gothic to represent how social worlds were constructed and constrained.

items.title: Reading and Representing Culture Through Foodways (Panel)
items.excerpt: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. This panel invites contributions that examine how food and foodways shape and reflect cultural knowledge across time and place, with particular attention to the relationships among foodways, memory, migration, identity, cultural heritage, and community formation. Pr...
items.fullDescription: Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. This panel invites contributions that examine how food and foodways shape and reflect cultural knowledge across time and place, with particular attention to the relationships among foodways, memory, migration, identity, cultural heritage, and community formation. Proposals are welcome from a range of disciplinary and professional perspectives that explore food and cultural identity in literature, cinema, and other forms of media; oral history projects; mapping initiatives; community archives and digital collections; public-facing scholarship; and pedagogical approaches to Food Studies. The panel also invites discussion of emerging technologies and methodologies that offer new ways of researching, representing, and engaging with foodways and cultural heritage, while expanding opportunities for the communication and exchange of cultural knowledge. Whether through literary and cinematic representation, historical research, oral histories, or other forms of cultural production and inquiry, food-centred scholarship offers valuable insights into the ways individuals and communities understand and communicate culture across generations and geographies. In keeping with the conference theme, Empowering Communities, this session considers how foodways can serve as sites of cultural expression, knowledge-sharing, and collective memory, while encouraging reflection on questions of belonging, exchange, continuity, and cultural change.

items.fullDescription: In this modern moment, there have been a myriad of proposals exploring the contours of “speculative fiction”—be that social, environmental, &c. This session seeks to deconstruct the ways in which the speculative shapes the contours of the “real” or realized. Though sensationalization can be found in works of fiction, the threads of one’s reality—intersectional as it may be—provides a rich narrative that encourages further exploration and the fears provided in what we would safely relegate to fiction. However, there remains a “seeping out” of those fears that can eerily be represented in the real world.

As such, this panel session invites papers that discuss the literary community of the Central Valley.

items.title: Ecologies of Attention: Literature and Pedagogies of the More-than-Human
items.excerpt: Today's ecological problems demand not only ecological awareness but also a fundamental rethinking of the human-nature relationship with the more-than-human world. Increased climatic change, species extinction, ecological degradation, and planetary instability. Challenge anthropocentric assumptions where humans are seen as separate from, superior to, or s...
items.fullDescription: Today's ecological problems demand not only ecological awareness but also a fundamental rethinking of the human-nature relationship with the more-than-human world. Increased climatic change, species extinction, ecological degradation, and planetary instability. Challenge anthropocentric assumptions where humans are seen as separate from, superior to, or sole interpreters of nature. This panel invites papers that explore how literature, storytelling, environmental humanities, and creative pedagogies might cultivate "ecologies of attention" within teacher education and educational spaces. Rather than approaching nature as a passive object of study or a resource for human learning, the session seeks to examine how literary and cultural texts unsettle human-centered ways of knowing and open possibilities for relational, more-than-human pedagogies. How might literature invite future educators to engage with ecological interconnectedness, multispecies agency, and nonhuman forms of intelligence, communication, and temporality? What pedagogical possibilities emerge when teachers and learners encounter forests, rivers, plants, animals, weather systems, and landscapes not as background settings, but as active participants within shared ecological worlds? How can educational practices move beyond extractive or instrumental approaches to environmental learning toward forms of attentiveness, reciprocity, care, and ethical coexistence? The panel is particularly interested in pedagogical and creative engagements that explore how literary and speculative texts reshape ecological imagination and challenge anthropocentric assumptions within teacher education, curriculum studies, literacy practices, and environmental pedagogy. Possible topics include, but are not limited to: Ecocriticism and teacher education Pedagogies of the more-than-human Literature and ecological attentiveness Climate fiction and environmental pedagogy Multispecies storytelling and relational learning Posthumanism and educational philosophy Ecological imagination in literacy education Biosemiotics, ecological communication, and pedagogy Plant studies, animal studies, and arboreal thinking in education Indigenous ecological knowledge and decolonial pedagogies Eco-poetry, speculative fiction, and climate narratives in the classroom Slow pedagogies, ecological temporality, and relational learning Environmental ethics and pedagogies of coexistence Creative-critical approaches to ecological teaching and learning Affective responses to climate anxiety, grief, and ecological uncertainty The curriculum, sustainability, and more than human relationality are explored. Reading practices that decenter the human subject. Ecological storytelling and pedagogies oriented towards the future. The session invites interdisciplinary, theoretical, pedagogical, creative-critical, and practice-based contributions from scholars, educators, teacher educators, creative writers, and Researchers in the fields of literature, education, environmental humanities, and cultural studies and related fields. Papers engaging works such as The Overstory, How I Became a Tree, The Ministry for the Future, The Grapes of Wrath, Climate Fiction, eco-poems, speculations, and Environmental media are particularly welcome, although not restricted to these texts. Submission Guidelines Please submit: An abstract of 250-300 words A short bio-note (100 words) Institutional affiliation (if applicable) Please send submissions to: mussaratshahid@fccollege.edu.pk

items.title: Call For Papers – NeMLA 2027 Panel -- Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time
items.excerpt: Call For Papers - NeMLA 2027 Panel -- Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time Northeast MLA 2027: March 6-9, 2027 Panel Title: Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time Modality: Virtual Only: All presentations will be delivered via Zoom regardless of whether the presenters are in-per...
items.fullDescription: Call For Papers - NeMLA 2027 Panel -- Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time Northeast MLA 2027: March 6-9, 2027 Panel Title: Supporting the Well-Being of Students and Instructors in a Transitional Time Modality: Virtual Only: All presentations will be delivered via Zoom regardless of whether the presenters are in-person Format: 3-4 presentations/papers, 15-20 minutes per presenter The past few years - which could be described as part of a larger cultural, political, and economic flash point -- have created true challenges for higher education. This transitional time has put pressure on all parties of our postsecondary community, as seen in the reported mental health challenges of undergraduates and the high rates of burnout amongst faculty and support networks in the sector (Mowreader; Pourasadollahi and Vincenti; Liu). In addition to these post-pandemic stressors, a host of broader concerns are also impinging on the postsecondary environment (see "The Trends Report" for one discussion). How can we empower our community - defined through instructors, students, and/or support services -- during such a transitional moment? This session will create a dialogue that touches on any of the following: -discussions/studies of interventions and/or articulations of already established practices in supporting the well-being of students and/or instructors; -the reflections of students, instructors, and/or support networks on the challenges of and responses to our contemporary moment. These challenges can include: the impact of budget cuts on postsecondary contexts, the fallout of declining academic freedom in the classroom, the impact (positive or negative) of Gen AI on the well-being of our community, the discord between instructor expectations and the reality of students. References Liu, Adrienne. "Higher Education is Exhausted." The Chronicle of Higher Education, 10 Feb. 2025, https://www.chronicle.com/article/higher-education-is-exhausted. Mowreader, Ashley. "Survey: Getting a Grip on the Student Mental Health Crisis," Inside Higher Ed, 19 Aug. 2024, https://www.insidehighered.com/news/student-success/health-wellness/2024/08/19/experts-weigh-college-student-mental-health-crisis. Pourasadollahi, Khatereh and Michele Vincenti. "Unveiling the Layers of Faculty Burnout in Canadian Private Universities during the post-COVID Pandemic Period." Journal of Education and Learning, vol. 14, no. 2, 2025, pp. 43-52. https://doi.org/10.5539/jel.v14n2p43. "The Trends Report 2026," The Chronicle of Higher Education, March 2026, https://www.chronicle.com/package/trends-report-2026. Submission Details 200-300 word abstract to the NeMLA portal (https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22639) by September 30, 2026 Please email any questions to Stacy Denton (sdenton@uwaterloo.ca)

items.title: CFP: Southern Italy beyond stereotypes and exoticization (Roundtable)
items.location: Newport, RI - Hybrid · Salve Regina University
items.excerpt: NeMLA 2027 March 6-9, 2027 Newport, RI Salve Regina University Southern Italy beyond stereotypes and exoticization (Roundtable, Hybrid) CFP https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22680 Southern Italy is often depicted stereotypically, both in Italian cultural production and in international social media content and blogs that promote La Dolce Vita as a desirabl...
items.fullDescription: NeMLA 2027 March 6-9, 2027 Newport, RI Salve Regina University Southern Italy beyond stereotypes and exoticization (Roundtable, Hybrid) CFP https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22680 Southern Italy is often depicted stereotypically, both in Italian cultural production and in international social media content and blogs that promote La Dolce Vita as a desirable lifestyle. While instructors can leverage some stereotypes and content in in-class activities to teach Italian language and culture, lower the affective filter, or introduce new topics and discussions, an excessive use of reels, songs, skits, films, and literature that play with such generalizations runs the risk of promoting a backward or distorted view of the country, its culture, and its communities, already affected by prejudice, overtourism and the touristification of entire neighborhoods or cities. In keeping with this year's conference theme, this roundtable invites short (5-10 minutes) presentations that explore ideas and content that convey the complexity of Italian culture and society beyond the stereotypes and the exoticization of Southern Italy, its cultures, places, communities, and bodies. Presentations may focus on pedagogical aspects, activities, and practices, or on primary sources used in the L2/LS and content classrooms to discuss any aspect of Italian culture and society without focusing on clichés related to the South (including Sicily, Sardinia, and the Mediterranean). Please submit a 200- 250-word abstract and a short bio through the NeMLA website (CFP https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22680) by September 30, 2026. For questions, please contact the roundtable organizers: Giuseppina Gemboni (ggemboni@andrew.cmu.edu), Angela Fabris (Angela.Fabris@aau.at).

items.title: The Body Remembers: Women, Trauma, and the Reclamation of Voice (Seminar)
items.excerpt: This session explores how women's bodies function as contested archives of trauma, sites of testimony that dominant social, legal and political structures have often silenced or delegitimised. Drawing on feminist trauma theory, postcolonial critique and affect studies, it examines bodily memory as a form of somatic knowledge that exceeds conventional lang...
items.fullDescription: This session explores how women's bodies function as contested archives of trauma, sites of testimony that dominant social, legal and political structures have often silenced or delegitimised. Drawing on feminist trauma theory, postcolonial critique and affect studies, it examines bodily memory as a form of somatic knowledge that exceeds conventional language and probes the tension between trauma's structural unspeakability and the ethical urgency of testimony and witnessing. The session invites work attentive to how race, caste, class, sexuality, coloniality and disability shape whose pain is heard and whose is erased. Relevant material includes autobiographical writing, testimonial literature, oral histories, visual and performance culture and collective protest/memorial practices with particular interest in South Asian Partition narratives and women's life writing as archives of embodied suffering and resistance. Theoretically grounded in scholars such as Judith Herman, Sara Ahmed, Veena Das, Shoshana Felman, Urvashi Butalia, Kamla Bhasin, Krishna Menon, and Dori Laub, the session also welcomes non-Western and vernacular frameworks that challenge Eurocentric trauma paradigms. Papers connecting individual testimony to systemic gendered violence, and theorising survival and creative expression as political resistance, are especially encouraged. The session calls for a feminist ethics of listening that honors women's embodied experience across geographies, resists pathologising traumatic memory and recognises speech from a wounded body as an act of defiance. Submissions: 250-300 word abstracts with a brief bio, from literary studies, history, sociology, gender studies, postcolonial studies, cultural studies, Dalit studies and allied fields. Modality: Hybrid- The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included.

items.title: Palestine and the Challenge to German Studies
items.location: Newport, Rhode Island · Northeast Modern Language Association (NeMLA)
items.excerpt: Palestine and the Challenge to German Studies Northeast Modern Language Association (NeMLA) annual convention Newport, Rhode Island March 6-9, 2027 Submission Deadline: 30 September 2026 through the NeMLA portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22528 Recent work in and around German Studies (Younes and Al-Taher; El Bulbeisi; Özyürek; Moses; Salehi)...
items.fullDescription: Palestine and the Challenge to German Studies Northeast Modern Language Association (NeMLA) annual convention Newport, Rhode Island March 6-9, 2027 Submission Deadline: 30 September 2026 through the NeMLA portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22528 Recent work in and around German Studies (Younes and Al-Taher; El Bulbeisi; Özyürek; Moses; Salehi) argues that the erasure of Palestinian identity in Germany is tied to deeper national tensions: How the country remembers its past, how it talks or avoids talking about race, what kinds of political speech are treated as acceptable, and how it confronts histories of imperialism and genocide. Moreover, scholars (Thompson and Tuzcu; Ragab; Daher) have documented how major institutions in Germany have been engaged in a wave of repression of ethnic minority communities, targeting Palestinians, other people of color, and Jewish anti-Zionists. This scholarship demonstrates how such pressures influence which voices, histories, and political positions are treated as legitimate within the field of German Studies, and which ones are marginalized. This panel invites contributions that explore how German Studies has begun or has struggled to engage with the significance of Palestine and Palestinian life, not only in contemporary German culture, politics, and literature, but within the discipline's own structures. While scholarship on Holocaust memory culture, as well as on multidirectional memory and migrant memory archives, has broadened the field's understanding of how Holocaust memory circulates and how migrant communities navigate its demands, recent work, such as articles by Catlin, Kundnani, and Rosenbrück in the New German Critique special issue "German Memory Politics at a Crossroads" (2026) provides evidence of the limits of these frameworks and the political pressures that shape them. Rather than challenge the centrality of Holocaust memory itself, this panel aims to examine how it is mobilized to foreclose other histories of violence, displacement, and coloniality, and how this foreclosing makes it difficult to integrate anti-imperialist, antiracist, and decolonial approaches that would situate Germany within broader histories of imperialism, colonial violence, and genocide. Palestine makes these dynamics visible by revealing how debates about memory shape disciplinary boundaries, how racialization and political speech determine scholarly legitimacy, and how exclusions are reproduced even within critical frameworks. This panel welcomes contributions that examine where Palestine appears, where it is actively excluded, and how these patterns expose German Studies' unresolved tensions around race, German colonial histories, and the politics of knowledge production. The goal of this panel is to open a conversation about how German Studies might expand beyond memory-centered approaches toward more globally attuned, anti?imperialist, and decolonial analytical frameworks. Topics include but are not limited to: · Intersectional decolonial feminist frameworks and praxes · Critiques of white Western imperial feminism · "Pinkwashing" and "homonationalism" to justify colonial violence and dehumanize Indigenous communities · The role of anti-Palestinian racism in mainstream anti-immigrant backlash and far-right political actors · Scapegoating of Muslims as the new Nazis in the theory of "imported anti-Semitism" (Özyürek) · Germany's colonial and neocolonial relationships to the broader region of West Asia · Integrating Palestinian perspectives into pedagogy, curriculum, and study abroad advising · Courses and lesson plans that include the Nakba and its continuity · German Studies and BDS/academic boycott · The "Historikerstreit 2.0" and attacks on "postcolonialism" · The history of East German solidarity with Palestinians · Student and faculty support in campus organizing for Palestine solidarity, and institutional and departmental barriers Abstracts: Please submit 300-word abstracts by 30 September 2026 though the NeMLA portal: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22528 For questions, please contact the panel organizer Maria Grewe (mgrewe@jjay.cuny.edu).

This unprecedented move in U.S. history threatened to erase millions of people from the map. It would reshape electoral outcomes, going against the 14th Amendment that requires counting all persons in each state to determine representation,...
items.fullDescription: A few months into his second term, President Trump announced he would order a new census that excludes non-citizens. This unprecedented move in U.S. history threatened to erase millions of people from the map. It would reshape electoral outcomes, going against the 14th Amendment that requires counting all persons in each state to determine representation, and it could impact public policymaking by removing crucial data for infrastructure, health, or education.

items.location: aspeers: emerging voices in american studies
items.excerpt: From its founding ideals framing rights as tied to individuals to many of the ‘myths that made America,’ such as the self-made man, ideas of individualism are deeply entrenched in the culture of the United States. Yet at the same time, the US has also always understood itself as a particular community: as a city upon a hill, a melting pot, a more perfect...
items.fullDescription: From its founding ideals framing rights as tied to individuals to many of the ‘myths that made America,’ such as the self-made man, ideas of individualism are deeply entrenched in the culture of the United States. Yet at the same time, the US has also always understood itself as a particular community: as a city upon a hill, a melting pot, a more perfect union. As such, the United States has been established on a seeming paradox, forming ‘out of many, one.’ More recently, this previous consensus has become increasingly brittle, with fragmentation, polarization, hyper-individualism, and loneliness on the rise—as some would argue: again.

items.title: Polish Science Fiction: Literature, Film, and Cultural Legacy
items.excerpt: Despite the international prominence of Stanisław Lem and, more recently, Andrzej Sapkowski's The Witcher , Polish science fiction and speculative literature remain strikingly underrepresented in English-language scholarship. Existing criticism tends to orbit a small number of canonical figures and a handful of frequently revisited texts, leaving a rich,...
items.fullDescription: Despite the international prominence of Stanisław Lem and, more recently, Andrzej Sapkowski's The Witcher , Polish science fiction and speculative literature remain strikingly underrepresented in English-language scholarship. Existing criticism tends to orbit a small number of canonical figures and a handful of frequently revisited texts, leaving a rich, historically complex tradition, spanning censorship-era political allegory, post-1989 transformation, contemporary literary crossover, and a distinct national cinema largely unexamined outside of Polish-language scholarship.

items.title: Exploring the Boundary Waters of the Blue Humanities
items.excerpt: Call for Papers: NANO: New American Notes Online (ISSN: 2160-0104) Special Issue 18: Exploring the Boundary Waters of the Blue Humanities Special Issue Guest Editors: Eric Denbang Kunkuri and Dana Van Kooy Deadline: 16 October 2026 Publication: spring/summer 2027 Full CFP Details: https://nanocrit.com/index.php/home/cfp-plus-submissions-new The creative a...
items.fullDescription: Call for Papers: NANO: New American Notes Online (ISSN: 2160-0104) Special Issue 18: Exploring the Boundary Waters of the Blue Humanities Special Issue Guest Editors: Eric Denbang Kunkuri and Dana Van Kooy Deadline: 16 October 2026 Publication: spring/summer 2027 Full CFP Details: https://nanocrit.com/index.php/home/cfp-plus-submissions-new The creative and scholarly work being done in the blue humanities has deepened our turn towards exploring waterways and boundary waters. The currents circulating through the blue humanities flow into and from myriad disciplines, including anthropology, biology, decolonial studies, feminist/queer studies, geography, history, indigenous studies, literature, music, performance studies, philosophy, and the visual arts. The cultural and theoretical inlets being carved out by work in the blue humanities have proven generative in terms of developing a distinctive metaphor-laden language, analytic methods like Kamau Brathwaite's theory of "tidalectics," and new ways of submerging ourselves in both historical and unprecedented questions about the water that is in and, as Rachel Carson notes in her famous title, "around us." For this special issue, we invite submissions of traditional and multimodal essays of up to 5,000 words (excluding works cited) that explore the boundary waters of the blue humanities. We encourage essays focused on narratives as well as submissions that explore art installations and other medial forms that address the boundary waters circulating around and through the blue humanities. Topics may include but are not limited to the following: Boundary waters that divide and connect nations, classes, and/or ecological zones Mythic, cinematic, and visual narratives of how humans and/or nonhumans are "held," migrate, or are moved through watery littorals Critical explorations like those associated with "tidalectics," a term that comes from Kamau Brathwaite, who proposed an alternative framework for literary and political engagement that is grounded in the rhythm of waves and the cyclic ebb and flow of tides "Portraits" of littoral space(s) in literature, history, myth, the visual arts, and theatrical and musical performances Exploring the significance of physical, structural, and emotional vulnerabilities and instabilities within littorals, shoals, or port cities Hydrological infrastructure (colonial, postcolonial, urban, rural) associated with port cities, canal locks, and dams Relational undercurrents that speak to our ways of submerging ourselves into the watery worlds that surround us Traditional water ways: ways of being with or "holding" water The role of inland waterways in routing stories and/or facilitating/impeding human and nonhuman migrations Exploring the agency of oceans and rivers in sustaining capitalist and imperial economies and their alternatives Water's many forms (snow, ice, fog, rivers, oceans) and ways "water" facilitates connection and/or imposes isolation How waterways-"paths" of water and ways of being with water-lead us to new or alternative futures Scenes of erosion that expose lost or forgotten histories Please direct questions to the special issue editors: Eric Denbang Kunkuri [edkunkur@mtu.edu] and Dana Van Kooy [drvankoo@mtu.edu] For questions about the use and disclosure of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools, please see NANO's AI Statement (https://nanocrit.com/index.php/Journal-Information) Please use the Submission Form on top left of the CFP page: https://nanocrit.com/index.php/Submissions/Submission-Form NANO: New American Notes Online is a free, open-access, peer-reviewed journal. NANO does not charge for submission, review, or publication. It is indexed in the MLA International Bibliography, GALE, ProQuest, and DOAJ.

items.title: TRANSPOSITIONES, Vol. 6, No. 2 (2027) Call for Papers: Earth / Sediment / Trace: Geologies, Material Memories, and the More-than-Living World
items.excerpt: TRANSPOSITIONES Journal for Transdisciplinary and Intermedial Culture Studies / Zeitschrift fu?r transdisziplinäre und intermediale Kulturforschung, ISSN 2749-4128 (print), 2749-4136 (online), Vol. 6, No. 2 (2027) Call for Papers Thematic focus of the issue: Earth / Sediment / Trace: Geologies, Material Memories, and the More-than-Living World Across the...
items.fullDescription: TRANSPOSITIONES Journal for Transdisciplinary and Intermedial Culture Studies / Zeitschrift fu?r transdisziplinäre und intermediale Kulturforschung, ISSN 2749-4128 (print), 2749-4136 (online), Vol. 6, No. 2 (2027) Call for Papers Thematic focus of the issue: Earth / Sediment / Trace: Geologies, Material Memories, and the More-than-Living World Across the environmental humanities, increasing attention has been devoted to the geological dimensions of culture and the cultural dimensions of the Earth. Geohumanities, critical mineral studies, energy humanities, environmental justice scholarship, and related fields have opened new perspectives on extraction, sedimentation, toxicity, waste, material memory, and deep time. At a moment marked by accelerating ecological transformations and growing awareness of the material infrastructures that sustain contemporary life, geological matter has emerged as a crucial site for rethinking history, memory, environment, and cultural imagination. Rather than treating the Earth as a passive backdrop to human activity, recent scholarship has approached rocks, minerals, soils, fossils, sediments, and other material formations as archives of planetary processes, environmental change, cultural memory, and political conflict. Questions of resource extraction, environmental justice, and slow violence increasingly intersect with inquiries into deep temporalities, material traces, sacred landscapes, and the long afterlives of human and more-than-human actions. This issue invites contributions that explore how literature, art, media, philosophy, religion, and cultural practices engage with geological matter, deep time, sedimented histories, and the material conditions of planetary life. We especially welcome work that investigates the cultural significance of rocks, minerals, fossils, soil, waste, ruins, and other forms of more-than-living matter, while also addressing questions of environmental justice, resource extraction, toxicity, and slow violence. We welcome analyses of literary, philosophical, historical, and cultural texts from a wide range of regions and traditions, including comparative and transnational perspectives. Topics may include: • geohumanities and geological imagination; • earth, stone, sediment, dust, and soil; • lithic ecologies and critical mineral studies; • deep time, planetary temporalities, and environmental history; • extractivism, mining, resource conflicts, and energy humanities; • environmental justice and slow violence; • toxic landscapes and contaminated environments; • waste, ruins, landfills, and material afterlives; • fossils, paleontology, and material histories of life; • material memory, archives, traces, and sedimented histories; • sacred geographies, holy mountains, stones, and pilgrimage landscapes; • archaeology, antiquarianism, and the materialities of the past; • textual strata, sedimented knowledge, and epistemic layers; • geological trauma, memory, and post-catastrophic landscapes; • petrofiction, climate fiction, and environmental storytelling; • artistic and literary engagements with earth materials; • relations between living and non-living matter; • fungi, decomposition, and processes of material transformation; • posthumanist, new materialist, and decolonial approaches to geology and the earth. Proposals comprising a 250-word abstract in English or German and a brief biographical note should be sent to: transpositiones@uw.edu.pl by October 20, 2026. A final decision regarding abstract acceptance will be made by October 31. Deadline for submitting completed manuscripts: February 28, 2027. The issue is expected to be published in the autumn of 2027.

items.title: Theory and Practice in Horror Transmedia Fandom and Creation
items.excerpt: Call for Chapters Proposals Submission Deadline: July 17, 2026 Full Chapters Due: October 30, 2026 Submission Date: October 30, 2026 Introduction This edited collection features scholarly voices with attention to horror in its transmedia representations, including film, literature, comics, art, branding, and more. Chapters give attention to the ways that...
items.fullDescription: Call for Chapters Proposals Submission Deadline: July 17, 2026 Full Chapters Due: October 30, 2026 Submission Date: October 30, 2026 Introduction This edited collection features scholarly voices with attention to horror in its transmedia representations, including film, literature, comics, art, branding, and more. Chapters give attention to the ways that fans take up the realities and ideas presented in horror, the affordances of the genre, and participation in this network of storytelling. The reactions of readers/viewers/fans, the decisions of creatives, the presentation of ideas within particular forms of media, and more form elements of the researched conversation in this volume. Objective This collection seeks to advance conversation about the theoretical functions of horror across media including notions of adaptation and literary/literacy-based choices in the work. This includes, but is not limited to, design structures, cultural influences, the role of lore, (re)considerations of the monstrous, embodiment in the genre, sub-genre considerations, and more. Target Audience This book is intended for academics in media, literacy, literature, communication, and interdisciplinary fields. Students enrolled in courses in these fields can benefit from this knowledge as they pursue courses of study and further academic work in these disciplines. Recommended Topics Emotional and Philosophical Reactions to Horror Media Themes and Interactions across Media Representations in the Genre Adaptation Processes/Decisions/Designs in Horror across Media Studies of Narrative and Response/Interpretation Recent Trends/Movements in Horror Theoretical Approaches to Horror Composition/Representation Analysis of Race, Gender, Class in Horror Considerations and Reconsiderations of the Monstrous Author/Creator Decisions and Purposes in the Genre/across Media Horror Character and Author/Director Studies Critical and Analytical Work on Embodiment in Horror Critical and Analytical Examinations of Cultural Stories in the Genre The Role of Lore/Mythology in Horror Studies of Writing/Creating Processes in the Genre Fandom Communities, Fan Fiction, Cosplay, and Other Responses Submission Procedure Researchers and practitioners are invited to submit on or before July 17, 2026, a chapter proposal of 1,000 to 2,000 words clearly explaining the mission and concerns of his or her proposed chapter. Authors will be notified by July 31, 2026 about the status of their proposals and sent chapter guidelines.Full chapters of a minimum of 10,000 words (word count includes references and related readings) are expected to be submitted by October 30, 2026, and all interested authors must consult the guidelines for manuscript submissions at https://www.igi-global.com/publish/contributor-resources/before-you-write/ prior to submission. All submitted chapters will be reviewed on a double-anonymized review basis. Contributors may also be requested to serve as reviewers for this project. Note: There are no submission or acceptance fees for manuscripts submitted to this book publication, Theory and Practice in Horror Transmedia Fandom and Creation. All manuscripts are accepted based on a double-anonymized peer review editorial process. All proposals should be submitted through the eEditorial Discovery® online submission manager. Publisher This book is scheduled to be published by IGI Global Scientific Publishing, an international academic publisher of the "Information Science Reference", "Medical Information Science Reference", "Business Science Reference", and "Engineering Science Reference" imprints. IGI Global Scientific Publishing specializes in publishing reference books, scholarly journals, and electronic databases featuring academic research on a variety of innovative topic areas including, but not limited to, education, social science, medicine and healthcare, business and management, information science and technology, engineering, public administration, library and information science, media and communication studies, and environmental science. For additional information regarding the publisher, please visit https://www.igi-global.com. This publication is anticipated to be released in 2027. Indexing Information for Prospective Authors IGI Global Scientific Publishing meets the criteria for inclusion in major indexing services such as Scopus; however, it is important to note that all indexing decisions are made independently by these services. IGI Global Scientific Publishing books are selectively indexed by the indexing organization after publication. Indexing cannot be guaranteed for any book prior to publication, and the indexing organization has complete control over the final selection and timeline. items.title: Edited Volume on Interrogating Indigenous Ecologies in Times of Crisis: Literature, Theory, and Praxis
items.location: Dr. Somasree Santra, Department of Language and Literature, Adamas University
items.excerpt: What is the real difference between speaking with nature and speaking for it? Is eco-centric thought a universally shared ideology, or does it develop in reaction to historical, philosophical, and socio-cultural contexts? In what way do the fundamental principles of Indian ecology vary from prevailing Western structures? These essential inquiries delineat...
items.fullDescription: What is the real difference between speaking with nature and speaking for it? Is eco-centric thought a universally shared ideology, or does it develop in reaction to historical, philosophical, and socio-cultural contexts? In what way do the fundamental principles of Indian ecology vary from prevailing Western structures? These essential inquiries delineate the parameters of Ramachandra Guha’s Speaking with Nature: The Origins of Indian Environmentalism (2024), which effectively contests the notion that environmentalism is a unidirectional phenomenon dictated by Western influence. This view posits that the rest of the world, particularly the Global South, lacks eco-conscious awareness because it is ‘too poor to be green’.

items.title: Developmentalism, Capitalism, and Vulnerable Lives: Reflection on Regional Literature in India
items.sourceName: UPenn CFP: Ecocriticism and Environmental Studies
items.excerpt: In India, the displacement of Adivasis, tribals, poor, farmers, forest-dwelling and land-based people, and other marginalized communities for development and infrastructure projects like dams, railways, power projects, and bridge construction, or for capitalist accumulation like mining, resource extraction, tourism, and industry, is a common occurrence. T...
items.fullDescription: In India, the displacement of Adivasis, tribals, poor, farmers, forest-dwelling and land-based people, and other marginalized communities for development and infrastructure projects like dams, railways, power projects, and bridge construction, or for capitalist accumulation like mining, resource extraction, tourism, and industry, is a common occurrence. The trajectory of such displacement has a long history, starting in the colonial period and being aggravated in the postcolonial period under the Nehruvian model of development. Although such projects are implemented for the sake of the ‘greater common good’ for all, only a few elites enjoy the benefit of these projects, depriving millions of displaced, marginalized people (Roy 1999).

items.title: New Approaches to Teaching the Gothic
items.excerpt: Diane Long Hoeveler and Tamar Heller's Approaches to Teaching Gothic Fiction: British and American Traditions (MLA Options for Teaching) was published in 2003; Anna Powell and Andrew Smith's Teaching the Gothic (Palgrave) was published in 2006. While both texts are excellent guides for teachers of the Gothic, quite a lot has happened in the twenty years s...
items.fullDescription: Diane Long Hoeveler and Tamar Heller's Approaches to Teaching Gothic Fiction: British and American Traditions (MLA Options for Teaching) was published in 2003; Anna Powell and Andrew Smith's Teaching the Gothic (Palgrave) was published in 2006. While both texts are excellent guides for teachers of the Gothic, quite a lot has happened in the twenty years since these texts appeared, not the least of which was a Gothic-infused global pandemic and the insidious rise of artificial intelligence in daily life. Academics today are and have been teaching the Gothic at a time when the world's problems - rising fascism, global conflict, climate change, the devaluation of Higher Education, among many others - make the world itself seem downright Gothic. This collection will seek to provide updated reflections, evaluations, and strategies on teaching the Gothic and utilising or creating Gothic pedagogy in the contemporary moment and beyond. Courses on or including the Gothic are taught in a wide variety of venues within and outside academia; settings include community colleges, traditional undergraduate programmes, graduate programmes, community centres, prisons and reformatories. Classrooms themselves vary greatly, from giant lecture halls to small upper-level seminars to fully online virtual spaces. Students of the Gothic come from all ages and backgrounds, and include native speakers of English and students acquiring English later in life. These contexts, venues, and audiences present different challenges for the instructor, and require unique approaches to make for successful learning experiences. We are seeking contributions from a range of scholars at different ranks and types of institutions (as well as outside of them) to present a broad understanding of the diversity of how we study and teach the Gothic today, and to provide new directions for the future. We already have interest from an academic press. Potential topics might include but are not restricted to one or a mixture of the following: Teaching the Gothic in the midst of a crisis (e.g. pandemic, war, from a distance or physically within such spaces) Teaching the Gothic outside of the university / in non-traditional settings Challenges of AI to teaching the Gothic The use of various digital platforms in teaching / student research Gothic and the archives in contemporary times What texts (and which editions) absolutely must be on any Gothic syllabus (and why)? Gothic forms (teaching beyond traditional formats, for instance) Gothic theory as pedagogy (in times of crisis particularly) Syllabus organizational strategies and rationales: Chronological? Thematic? Canon? Decolonizing the Gothic syllabus Preparing for and managing class discussions about violence, sexual violence, trauma, discrimination, or other tough topics that arise in the Gothic? Strategies for teaching a Gothic course for an audience unfamiliar with the Gothic / unfamiliar with academia / non-traditional students. Gothic and public engagement / school outreach Opportunities for service learning / personal and professional development in the Gothic course Creative forms of assessment in the Gothic classroom Neurodivergence and the Gothic Designing assignments with AI in mind Cancel culture and the Gothic Co-editors Bridget Marshall (University of Massachusetts, Lowell) and Jen Baker (University of Warwick, UK) seek abstracts of 300-500 words for chapters which will demonstrate innovative approaches to teaching the Gothic or engaging with or creating new Gothic pedagogies, particularly situated within recent contexts. We are open to a variety of forms, including case studies, reflective essays, theoretically informed essays, and other formats that can best showcase pedagogical insights. If your proposed essay includes samples of work by students/participants we will require written permissions before publication. Please also include a brief professional bio of 300 words (max) with your abstract. Please send your abstract and bio by 10 November 2026 to Bridget_Marshall@uml.edu and J.Baker.5@warwick.ac.uk We are happy to answer questions at the same address.

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers - Volume 37, Issue 1 & 2 (2027) Dear Colleagues, Litera: Journal of Language, Literature and Culture Studies - Dil, Edebiyat ve Kültür Ara?t?rmalar? Dergisi, the official publication of the Department of Western Languages and Literatures at Istanbul University, invites submissions for its 2026 issues (Volume 36, Issues 1 & 2). An open-access, peer-reviewed, and multilingual journal published twice a year (June & December), Litera is indexed in ELSEVIER SCOPUS, Web of Science ESCI, MLA International Bibliography, DOAJ (Directory of Open Access Journals), ERIH PLUS, EBSCO Communication Source, EBSCO Central & Eastern European Academic Source, Gale Cengage and Ulrichsweb. While our core focus remains on literary studies, linguistics, and cultural studies, we welcome interdisciplinary approaches that combine literary and linguistic analysis with insights from cultural theory and media studies-provided they maintain a clear relevance to our aim and scope We welcome original research articles that engage with current debates and critical issues in the study of Western (American, English, French, Belgian, German, Swiss, Austrian, Italian, Spanish, Latin American and Modern Greek) languages, literatures, and cultures. In particular, we encourage contributions that reflect cutting-edge scholarship in areas such as, but not limited to: · Contemporary literary theory · Disability Studies · Human-Animal Studies · Posthumanism and critical posthumanities · Ecofeminism and ecocriticism · Ecosemiotics and Ecolinguistics · Discourse Studies · Intersectional and decolonial approaches · Digital humanities and media theory · Film Studies · Medieval Studies · Posthuman Communication Studies · Gender studies · Narrative and identity in contemporary fiction · Transnationalism and migration literature · Crete studies Submission Windows: · Issue 1 (June 2027): November 1-30, 2026 · Issue 2 (December 2027): May 1-31, 2027 Submission Guidelines: Manuscripts must be original, unpublished, and not under review elsewhere. Re-use of thesis content, dissertations, or previously published conference material is not permitted. All submissions are subject to a double-blind peer reviewprocess. Languages of Publication: English, German, French, Italian, and Spanish No processing or publication fees. Eligibility: Submissions are accepted from authors who hold a PhD. For more information, visit our journal homepage. We look forward to receiving your innovative and thought-provoking research. Sincerely, Editorial Team Litera: Journal of Language, Literature and Culture Studies

To achieve this, we require your active partnership in building...
items.fullDescription: Journal of Studies in Language, Culture and Society (JSLCS) ISSN / E-ISSN: 2716-9189 / 2676-1750 Publisher: University of Bejaia Editor-in-chief: Prof. Nadia Idri, Author Guidelines for JSLCS Standards Our Goal: To be a leading international, multidisciplinary forum for high-quality research. To achieve this, we require your active partnership in building a rigorous, visible, and influential scholarly community. 1. Core Philosophy: Quality, Originality, and International Contribution To be considered, your manuscript must make a clear, original, and significant contribution to its field on an international level. We prioritize research that engages with global scholarly conversations, not just regional or national discussions. Ask yourself: Why would a scholar on another continent find this paper important? 2. Abstract: Your International Showcase The abstract is your most critical tool for attracting an international readership. Length & Detail: Provide a structured abstract of 300-350 words. Use clear headings: Objective, Methodology, Results, Conclusion, Significance/Contribution. Content: Explicitly state the paper's novelty and its significance to the international research community. Avoid generic statements. Specify the gap you fill and how your findings advance the field globally. Language: Must be in excellent, native-level English. Consider professional editing if needed. This is non-negotiable for discoverability. 3. Manuscript Scope & "The Identity Challenge" JSLCS is multidisciplinary, but your paper must have a sharp, focused identity. Connect to Core Themes: While we cover language, culture, and society, your manuscript must clearly fit within one or two defined sub-fields. Justify Broad Relevance: If your topic is regionally specific (e.g., a case study in Algeria), the Introduction and Conclusion must explicitly articulate its theoretical, methodological, or comparative implications for international research. Avoid being "a paper of everything": We will desk-reject manuscripts that are overly broad, lack a clear theoretical framework, or do not connect to specific scholarly debates. 4. References & Engagement with the Global Scholarly Community Your reference list is a map of your scholarly engagement and is crucial for our global profile. Volume & Recency: A minimum of 25-30 high-quality references is expected. A significant majority should be from the last 5 years, demonstrating engagement with current discourse. Source Quality: Prioritize references from Scopus/Web of Science-indexed journals and reputable international academic presses (Routledge, Springer, Palgrave, John Benjamins, etc.). International Dialogue: Your literature review must engage with key international scholars and seminal works in your field, not just literature from a single region. Style: Strict adherence to APA 7th Edition. 5. Authorship & Affiliation International Collaboration: Research conducted by international teams or comparing multiple contexts is highly encouraged. Institutional Email & ORCID: All corresponding authors must provide an institutional email address and a valid ORCID ID. This is mandatory for submission. Author Profiles: Authors are encouraged to maintain active, public scholarly profiles (e.g., Google Scholar, Scopus Author ID, institutional webpage). 6. Language and Accessibility for an International Audience The full manuscript must be submitted in English. To enhance accessibility, we recommend that authors of highly regionally-focused studies consider including an extended summary (500-600 words) in the article's appendix, detailing the context and international relevance for non-specialists. 7. Ethical Rigor & Transparency AI Use Disclosure: Any use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools in the writing, editing, or analysis process must be transparently declared in the methodology or acknowledgments section. Describe the tool used and its purpose. AI cannot be listed as an author. Plagiarism Check: All submissions will be screened. Similarity must be below 20% (excluding references). Ethical Compliance: Studies involving humans must include proof of ethics approval. Submission Checklist: Before submitting via ASJP, ensure your manuscript: Makes a clear international contribution. Has a detailed, structured abstract highlighting novelty and significance. Fits a defined niche within our broad scope. Contains 25-30+ references (can be more), mostly recent and from high-indexed sources. Engages with international literature. Is written in excellent, polished English. Uses the correct template and APA 7th style. Includes author ORCID iD and institutional email. Has a declaration on AI use (if applicable). Submission via ASJP https://asjp.cerist.dz/en/PresentationRevue/681 More information: https://univ-bejaia.dz/jslcs

items.title: The Politics of Cute
items.excerpt: Cuteness is a twenty-first-century sensation—from the global expansion of Sanrio fandom to the rise of social capital around Labubu and Jellycat, from K-Pop stars (real or animated) to cute animals like the quokka and Fennec fox. This issue explores a “cuteness turn” found in many public realms around the world. The adoration of attractive or cheerful obj...
items.fullDescription: Cuteness is a twenty-first-century sensation—from the global expansion of Sanrio fandom to the rise of social capital around Labubu and Jellycat, from K-Pop stars (real or animated) to cute animals like the quokka and Fennec fox. This issue explores a “cuteness turn” found in many public realms around the world. The adoration of attractive or cheerful objects associated with childishness and youthfulness is part of the human experience. However, this issue does not examine cuteness as a transcultural aesthetic or psychological condition, but we focus on its larger political implications. Throughout history and around the world, this “universal” sentiment has often been manipulated for commercial and ideological purposes.

items.title: Star Wars at 50: Forces, Forms, Legacies
items.sourceName: UPenn CFP: Fan Studies and Fandom
items.excerpt: 2027 marks the 50th anniversary of the film now generally known as Episode IV: A New Hope , the first instalment in the hugely successful Star Wars franchise created by George Lucas. As beloved as it is divisive, Star Wars now straddles multiple decades and generations while proliferating across narrative media (novels, comics, games, animation, TV). It p...
items.fullDescription: 2027 marks the 50th anniversary of the film now generally known as Episode IV: A New Hope , the first instalment in the hugely successful Star Wars franchise created by George Lucas. As beloved as it is divisive, Star Wars now straddles multiple decades and generations while proliferating across narrative media (novels, comics, games, animation, TV). It provides a series of compelling case studies in the relationship between creativity and commerce, from the foundation of Lucasfilm during the New Hollywood period to the 21st century Disney-era, and it has developed via a complex interplay between cutting-edge technological innovation, nostalgia, and mythmaking.

items.title: Southern Humanities Conference, Knoxville TN, January 28-31, 2027
items.excerpt: The Southern Humanities Conference, 2027 Call for Papers Conference Theme: Divergence, Convergence, Emergence Knoxville, TN, January 28-31, 2027 The Southern Humanities Conference offers an opportunity for scholars, artists, writers, musicians, performers, and humanists of all kinds to share their knowledge, research, work, and experiences in an interdisc...
items.fullDescription: The Southern Humanities Conference, 2027 Call for Papers Conference Theme: Divergence, Convergence, Emergence Knoxville, TN, January 28-31, 2027 The Southern Humanities Conference offers an opportunity for scholars, artists, writers, musicians, performers, and humanists of all kinds to share their knowledge, research, work, and experiences in an interdisciplinary, welcoming, and engaging intellectual space. For 2027 our conference theme will be "Divergence, Convergence, Emergence." Divergent thinking is a hallmark of creative endeavors ranging from the visual and performative arts to scientific inquiry. It can also signify a transgression of a custom or standard, or even a social more or behavioral norm, through contravention or subversion of what others consider "ordinary" or "natural." On the other hand, the benefits of group diversity are undeniably numerous and valuable in education, collaboration, and problem-solving. Convergent thinking can help triangulate resolutions to mysteries in historical systems and literary plot structures, among other questions. Convergence can also be considered a precursor to meaningful communal and cooperative social and cultural interactions. The notion of emergence-a phenomenon in which novel, unpredictable, and sometimes unexpected outcomes arise from a constellation of interactions between simpler underlying elements-provides a way of understanding origins and evolutions of phenomena that interest the analytical and imaginative mind. Emergence is also often associated with the concept of synthesis and the idea that the whole can be greater than the sum of its parts. The intersections of (and interstices between) emergence, divergence, and convergence serve as the stimulus for our collective conversation in Knoxville and offers us a platform for appraising the world around us. The Southern Humanities Conference invites proposals for papers on any aspect of the theme "Divergence, Convergence, Emergence" broadly conceived. Our conference themes are meant to be inspiring and prompt reflection, not limiting. The topic is interdisciplinary and invites proposals from all areas of study, as well as creative pieces including but not limited to performance, music, art, and literature. Customary paper and full panel proposals are invited, as are ones for creative presentation formats like roundtables and demonstrations. Moreover, the Southern Humanities Conference welcomes proposals from teachers and professionals outside the academy, as well as from scholars in the early stages of their academic careers. Please note that the name of our organization simply reflects its having been founded in the U.S. South; no presenter is expected to present anything "southern," though southern topics are also welcomed. Conference attendees come from all over the United States, Canada, and overseas. SUBMISSION GUIDELINES: Please submit proposals of 300-500 words through our website at www.southernhumanities.org (preferred), or by email sent to Brett Bebber at southernhumanities@gmail.com. Proposals are due by December 1, 2026, but are accepted and reviewed on a rolling basis. We are currently planning to meet for an in-person conference in Knoxville. Conference registration fees for the 2027 Conference will be $195 for students and retirees and $250 for academic faculty and other professionals. CONFERENCE AWARDS: Two awards of up to $300 are available to help mitigate the costs of travel to the conference. The Kathryn M. Reynolds award is for first- or second-time presenters, and Bennie D. Ussery award is for the best proposed paper from a graduate student. A third award, the Mark Ledbetter and Susan Cumings Award, offers registration remission for up to four graduate students. If you would like to be considered for one of these awards, please indicate which award(s) in your proposal. Those interested in being considered for either award should submit their proposals by October 31, 2026.

items.title: “WETLANDS”: Representing and Historicizing Wetland Environments
items.excerpt: In the wake of ecocriticism and environmental humanities, the blue humanities have emerged as a field of study that emphasizes the centrality of aquatic environments in understanding interactions between humans and nonhumans. This interdisciplinary field, which initially grew out of Anglophone literary criticism, proposes to shift our terra-centric perspe...
items.fullDescription: In the wake of ecocriticism and environmental humanities, the blue humanities have emerged as a field of study that emphasizes the centrality of aquatic environments in understanding interactions between humans and nonhumans. This interdisciplinary field, which initially grew out of Anglophone literary criticism, proposes to shift our terra-centric perspective by adopting the seas and oceans as a new vantage point to rethink our understanding of both the planet and literature (Klein, 2002; Blum, 2008; Bailyn, 2005). Originally developed in the United States within the field of Oceanic studies (Blum 2010; Cohen 2010; Mentz 2009), the “Blue studies” have since expanded and become increasingly decentered and diversified.

items.fullDescription: "Making Trouble: Subversions and Reclamations in American Imaginaries" RIAS Vol. 21, Fall-Winter (2/2028) This issue of RIAS invites contributions on the theme of “Making Trouble: Subversions and Reclamations in American Imaginaries.” Engaging ongoing debates in global American Studies on power, representation, dissent, and cultural transformation, the issue seeks contributions that examine how American cultural, political, and social imaginaries are challenged, reworked, and reclaimed across diverse historical and geographical contexts. items.fullDescription: Call for Papers Social Lens (Volume 3, Issue 2 (2026) Social Lens (ISSN: 3078-3941 (Online), 3078-3933 (Print) is an international, peer-reviewed, open-access scholarly journal committed to advancing innovative, interdisciplinary, and evidence-based research across the broad spectrum of the social sciences. The journal provides a global platform for researchers, academicians, practitioners, and policymakers to disseminate original research that contributes to contemporary academic discourse and informs policy and practice. Themes of Interest Submissions are welcomed in, but are not limited to, the following areas: Sociology and Social Theory Political Science and Public Policy International Relations and Global Governance Economics and Development Studies Psychology and Behavioral Sciences Education and Educational Policy Anthropology and Cultural Studies Media and Communication Studies Public Administration and Governance Law and Society Gender and Women's Studies Human Geography and Urban Studies Peace, Conflict, and Security Studies Environmental and Sustainability Studies Public Health and Society Migration and Diaspora Studies Digital Society and Emerging Technologies Artificial Intelligence and Social Change Human Rights and Social Justice Data Science Applications in Social Sciences Interdisciplinary Social Science Research Types of Manuscripts The journal accepts: Original Research Articles Review Articles Why Publish with SSR? Double-Blind Peer Review Open Access Publication Rapid Editorial Decision DOI Assignment for Published Articles Immediate Online Publication upon Acceptance Global Visibility and Academic Reach No Submission Fee Submission Guidelines Authors should ensure that: Manuscripts are original and not under consideration elsewhere. Submissions follow the journal's author guidelines. Proper citation and referencing standards are maintained. Similarity and AI-generated content comply with the journal's publication ethics. All manuscripts undergo editorial screening followed by double-blind peer review. Important Dates Submission Opens: Immediately Submission Deadline:Rolling Basis Notification of Review Decision: Rolling Basis Publication: Upon Acceptance How to Submit Authors should submit their manuscripts through the journal's online submission system available at: https://socialsciencesresearch.com For queries regarding submissions or publication, please contact the editorial office through the journal website.

items.title: International Journal of English Literature, Linguistics, and Social Sciences: A Multidisciplinary Journal
items.excerpt: The International Journal of English Literature, Social Sciences, and Multidisciplinary Studies (IJELS) publishes original research papers, review articles, and case studies that advance scholarly knowledge across English literature, social sciences, and multidisciplinary studies. The journal provides a global platform for researchers, educators, and prof...
items.fullDescription: The International Journal of English Literature, Social Sciences, and Multidisciplinary Studies (IJELS) publishes original research papers, review articles, and case studies that advance scholarly knowledge across English literature, social sciences, and multidisciplinary studies. The journal provides a global platform for researchers, educators, and professionals to share innovative ideas, critical analyses, and interdisciplinary perspectives. Submission open to Current Issue Feedback Notification: within 1 Month after submission (Online Peer Review System) IJELS welcomes high-quality submissions on a broad range of topics, including but not limited to: English Literature and Language Studies - English literature, world literature, comparative literature, interdisciplinary approaches to literature, literature and media, critical theory, English language teaching and learning, communicative language teaching (CLT), task-based language teaching (TBLT), second language acquisition, English language testing and assessment, syllabus design and curriculum development, applied linguistics, corpus linguistics, discourse analysis, translation studies, English globalization, and English-speaking cultures. Social Sciences and Humanities - Anthropology, Sociology, Psychology, Political Science, Economics, Education, Geography, History, Law, International Relations, Criminology, Public Administration, Development Studies, Human Rights, Social Welfare, Gender Studies, and Ethics. Arts and Cultural Studies - Performing Arts (music, theatre, and dance), Visual Arts, Cultural and Cross-Cultural Studies, Media Studies, Communication Studies, and Philosophy. Business, Law, and Information Studies - Business Studies, Corporate Governance, Industrial Relations, Information Science, Library Science, and Paralegal Studies. Multidisciplinary Studies - Research that bridges multiple academic disciplines to address complex social, cultural, linguistic, educational, technological, or global challenges. IJELS particularly values cross-cutting studies that integrate methodologies and perspectives from different fields to generate new insights and foster innovation. IJELS encourages diverse theoretical, empirical, and methodological approaches that promote intellectual exchange and interdisciplinary collaboration, fostering research at the dynamic intersection of literature, language, social sciences, and multidisciplinary inquiry. You may submit your article to: ijels.infogain@gmail.com

items.title: A New Existentialism?
items.excerpt: How is everyone doing? At a routine meeting of the Editorial Board of this journal in March 2026, this innocuous question erupted into disquiet, tension, unease. Speaking from vastly different parts of the world, there was an unmistakable sense of a planet slipping beyond available comprehension and unplugging us from work and life routines. As wars in th...
items.fullDescription: How is everyone doing? At a routine meeting of the Editorial Board of this journal in March 2026, this innocuous question erupted into disquiet, tension, unease. Speaking from vastly different parts of the world, there was an unmistakable sense of a planet slipping beyond available comprehension and unplugging us from work and life routines. As wars in the Middle East expand and fires spread from apartment blocks reduced to rubble to bombed oil refineries and shipping corridors, profound geopolitical, personal, even psychic rifts leave us grasping for the language and feel of the moment, the falling apart of things, the zeitgeist of an endangered now.

items.title: JMMLA CFP Spring 2027: Computation, Interdisciplinarity, and the Humanities
items.excerpt: The advancement of artificial intelligence has transformed humanities research and education, deepening computation’s influence on scholarly practice and everyday life. From the early era of “humanities computing” in the 1970s to the rise of “computational humanities” over the past decade, this trajectory highlights the enduring—and expanding—role of comp...
items.fullDescription: The advancement of artificial intelligence has transformed humanities research and education, deepening computation’s influence on scholarly practice and everyday life. From the early era of “humanities computing” in the 1970s to the rise of “computational humanities” over the past decade, this trajectory highlights the enduring—and expanding—role of computation in shaping inquiry across the humanities. These intersections are especially visible in interdisciplinary work. As T. S. Eliot observes, “Immature poets imitate; mature poets steal; bad poets deface what they take, and good poets make it into something better, or at least something different.” The same spirit can illuminate how methods and tools migrate across fields.

items.title: Delta Ecologies
items.excerpt: Delta Ecologies asks how delta worlds are shaped by water’s movement, land’s instability, infrastructural promises, and the public stories through which communities remember loss, claim responsibility, and imagine survival. This issue begins from the Bengal-Gangetic world--especially the Ganges-Brahmaputra-Meghna delta, where river systems meet the Bay of...
items.fullDescription: Delta Ecologies asks how delta worlds are shaped by water’s movement, land’s instability, infrastructural promises, and the public stories through which communities remember loss, claim responsibility, and imagine survival. This issue begins from the Bengal-Gangetic world--especially the Ganges-Brahmaputra-Meghna delta, where river systems meet the Bay of Bengal--but it treats the delta not simply as a vulnerable climate zone. It understands the delta as a public world where environmental change becomes a matter of shared interpretation, memory, and responsibility.
