items.title: Musical Commemorations and Contestations in Postcolonial Contexts
items.excerpt: Public Humanities is an international open-access, cross-disciplinary, peer-reviewed journal at the intersection of humanities scholarship and public life. The journal invites submissions for the upcoming issue, Musical Commemorations and Contestations in Postcolonial Contexts, which will be guest edited by Bart Paul Vanspauwen, Ana-María Alarc&oac...
items.fullDescription: Public Humanities is an international open-access, cross-disciplinary, peer-reviewed journal at the intersection of humanities scholarship and public life. The journal invites submissions for the upcoming issue, Musical Commemorations and Contestations in Postcolonial Contexts, which will be guest edited by Bart Paul Vanspauwen, Ana-María Alarcón-Jiménez, and Livia Jiménez Sedano. Deadline for submissions: 1 February 2027 Description This special issue examines how sonic and kinetic practices-music and dance-are mobilised, contested, and governed within postcolonial commemorative and institutional contexts. It centres communities whose musical and choreographic traditions are routinely staged, suppressed, or appropriated by state institutions and heritage bureaucracies, bringing scholarship on chronically under-represented practices into a single, freely accessible comparative framework across language spheres and colonial legacies. The collection asks how music and dance function within-or in dialogue with-transnational institutions designed to consolidate language-based cultural systems, focusing on commemorative occasions and the sonic contestations they generate. From a hegemonic perspective, such commemorations produce collective memories that reproduce post-imperial geographies. From a critical perspective, they enact a persistent coloniality in which colonial-era categories endure in commemorative form. Submissions will be assessed on their engagement with one or more of the following: Musical and dance performance at official commemorative events Indigenous or colonial remembrance through music and dance Postcolonial justice and reparation via musical expression Tensions between state-sponsored and grassroots practices The instrumentalisation of intangible cultural heritage Decolonial approaches to commemoration The strongest submissions will offer a comparative or theoretically grounded account of how official institutions mediate culture through sound and movement, and what alternative practices emerge in response. Two original concepts anchor the issue: Sonic and kinetic governance: states' use of music and dance to construct authorised collective memory. Sonic and kinetic resistance: communities' use of the same media to contest that governance. The issue welcomes a diversity of perspectives and especially encourages submissions from early career researchers. It carries an explicit social mission aligned with international agendas on cultural heritage, inclusive societies, and accountable institutions, giving it purchase beyond academic audiences alone. Submission Guidelines Articles should be no more than 7,000 words, excluding the abstract, footnotes, and bibliography. Submissions should be written in accessible language for a wide readership across and beyond the humanities: see How to Write for Public Humanities. Authors should consult the journal's Author Instructions prior to submission. All authors must declare any funding and/or competing interests upon submission (see the journal's Publishing Ethics guidelines for more information). Articles will be peer reviewed for both content and style. Accepted articles will be published digitally as open access in the journal. Contacts The editor is happy to discuss potential articles: Bart Paul Vanspauwen bvanspauwen@fcsh.unl.pt Questions regarding peer review can be sent to: publichumanities@cambridge.org Submit an Article All submissions should be made through the Public Humanities online peer review system. Learn more at cambridge.org/pub

items.title: Learning Experience Design Conference
items.location: online · "L&D initiatives" Nataliia Gach Education Centre
items.excerpt: Our conference is a global online gathering of educators, instructional designers, learning innovators, researchers, and industry leaders dedicated to shaping the future of learning. LXD has emerged as a powerful bridge between learning design and educational technology, combining pedagogical expertise with technological innovation to create engaging, lea...
items.fullDescription: Our conference is a global online gathering of educators, instructional designers, learning innovators, researchers, and industry leaders dedicated to shaping the future of learning. LXD has emerged as a powerful bridge between learning design and educational technology, combining pedagogical expertise with technological innovation to create engaging, learnercentred experiences. This conference provides a unique platform for sharing cutting-edge practices, innovative approaches, and emerging trends in LXD across diverse educational and professional contexts. The conference programme includes keynote presentations, interactive workshops, panel discussions, session meetings and networking opportunities. We invite contributions from the following fields: 1) Learning Experience Design Foundations; 2) Learning Sciences and Pedagogy; 3) EdTech and Digital Innovation; 4) AI and the Future of Learning; 5) User Experience and Interface Design for Learning; 6) Workplace Learning and Professional Development; 7) Inclusion, Equity, and Accessibility. PARTICIPATION Conference language is English. To participate in the conference, please fill in a registration form on our website https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/ by March 05, 2027. The confirmation of conference participation will be sent within two weeks from the submission date but not later than March 12, 2027. Conference fee: 100 CHF. For participation in the conference both speakers and listeners will be awarded with ECTS credits (1 ECTS credit for speakers and 0,5 ECTS credits for listeners), which will be stated in the certificate of attendance. Certificates of attendance will be issued to all participants, including speakers and attendees, contingent upon their physical presence at the conference. REQUIREMENTS FOR SPEAKERS Agenda: plenary speeches - 30 minutes, section speeches - 15 minutes; discussion time - 10 minutes. PUBLICATION OF ABSTRACTS All conference participants may submit abstracts for publication. Abstracts will be published in the e-Book of Abstracts containing ISBN and DOI numbers. The language of publication is English. The deadline for abstracts submission is March 05, 2027. Abstracts must be downloaded in the registration form on our website: https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/. The abstract must contain 250-300 words, excluding references. The abstract must be preceded by the name(s) of the author(s), including their title(s), affiliation, and country (all in the abovementioned order). The title of the abstract must be in bold, all capitals, centered. References, if applicable (presented in the end of the abstract), and in-text citations must conform to the APA Style. All submitted abstracts will be subjected to the double-blind peer review process. The editorial board reserves the right to decline papers that contain plagiarized content or do not conform to the norms and subject matter of the conference. ENQUIRIES For any questions concerning the conference, please contact Dr. Nataliia Gach at info@ldinitiatives.ch or by phone: +41 78 340 32 13. For more information visit our website https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/.

items.title: Superhero-Con Symposium
items.excerpt: The rise of the superhero is one of the most significant cultural developments of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Since the debut of Superman in Action Comics #1 (April 18, 1938), superheroes have evolved from colorful comic-book figures into powerful global symbols that shape conversations about politics, morality, identity, technology, gender,...
items.fullDescription: The rise of the superhero is one of the most significant cultural developments of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Since the debut of Superman in Action Comics #1 (April 18, 1938), superheroes have evolved from colorful comic-book figures into powerful global symbols that shape conversations about politics, morality, identity, technology, gender, race, mythology, trauma, and resistance. What began with the imaginative vision of Jerry Siegel (writer) and Joe Shuster (artist) has expanded into a worldwide phenomenon spanning comics, film, television, literature, gaming, digital media, and academic discourse.

items.title: Subtle Performances of the Body: Female Agency and Autonomy in Literature
items.excerpt: How do female characters employ their bodies to navigate, process, and challenge the cultural and socioeconomic structures of their times? Under the antagonistic system where the pursuit of “good life” is no longer plausible, how do women across different cultures, historical periods, and geopolitical contexts respond by utilizing their bodily autonomy an...
items.fullDescription: How do female characters employ their bodies to navigate, process, and challenge the cultural and socioeconomic structures of their times? Under the antagonistic system where the pursuit of “good life” is no longer plausible, how do women across different cultures, historical periods, and geopolitical contexts respond by utilizing their bodily autonomy and agency? This panel invites papers that critically investigate female bodily representation in the context of empowerment and change. In particular, this panel is interested in literary analyses that focus on the subtle yet tangible (dis)orientation of female bodies in and out of public spaces.

items.title: Gender and the History of Institutions
items.excerpt: The aim of this book series is to examine the history of institutions around the world through the lens of gender. Of interest are institutions established with the specific purpose of regulating gender and sexuality (e.g. the Magdalen asylums, Magdalen hospitals, penitentiaries, refuges, mother and baby institutions) as well as those with more general pu...
items.fullDescription: The aim of this book series is to examine the history of institutions around the world through the lens of gender. Of interest are institutions established with the specific purpose of regulating gender and sexuality (e.g. the Magdalen asylums, Magdalen hospitals, penitentiaries, refuges, mother and baby institutions) as well as those with more general purposes where gender has had an important role in their operation and function (e.g. prisons, workhouses, lunatic asylums), including both religious and private organisations. The series supports the increasing interest in these institutions internationally, both in academia and in the treatment of ‘historical’ abuse.

items.title: BRAIN Focus Series
items.excerpt: Designed by Jean-François Vernay, the Routledge Literary BRAIN (Brain-Related Academic Investigations of Narratives) Focus Series combines the language of literary criticism with neurocognitive and health humanities methodologies or explanatory frameworks, providing an innovative way of blending literary analysis with health humanities and neurocognitive...
items.fullDescription: Designed by Jean-François Vernay, the Routledge Literary BRAIN (Brain-Related Academic Investigations of Narratives) Focus Series combines the language of literary criticism with neurocognitive and health humanities methodologies or explanatory frameworks, providing an innovative way of blending literary analysis with health humanities and neurocognitive approaches. This exciting BRAIN series is designed to convene conversations across interdisciplinary knowledges, covering all fiction and nonfiction sub-genres such as poetry, drama, novels, short-stories, memoirs, (auto)biographies, essays, etc.
